title: LLVM: Symbolic labels, comment cleanup, and label shift bug fix
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Documenting completed work.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
COMPLETED: All work done across 7 commits (ec2df44 through 411f2ec):

1. Symbolic JP/CALL labels: ADDR_TO_LABEL pre-pass maps 36,895 addresses to label names. JP/CALL now use symbolic names instead of hex addresses.

2. Drop redundant ASL comments: Native instructions no longer carry auto-generated comments restating the mnemonic. Same for .ascii/.asciz.

3. Symbolic .long: 9,766 numeric .long values replaced with symbolic label references.

4. Label-only segment labels: 4,344 emitted inline + 7,503 via .set. Content LABEL_XXXXXX on instruction lines use .set (103 cases).

5. DW label arithmetic: 30 dispatch table entries converted to symbolic A-B form across 3 dispatch tables.

6. Label shift bug fix: Data directives at exhausted block boundaries now get re-converted after block transition, fixing shifted label content (e.g. FILETYPE_SIG labels).

All changes verified: 100% byte-identical ROM match.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Additional work: Expanded .long symbolization from 9,766 to 15,089 references (commit a1561fe). Enhanced pre-pass tracks addresses through data directives and binclude files. Split into reliable (JP/CALL) and all (.long) maps to prevent instruction sizing errors from corrupting byte-matching.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Additional: moved address comments to label lines, split mixed byte/string DB lines (commit 7a7bc16). 100% byte-match preserved.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Added comprehensive LLVM fallback report (commit 857a75f): 126,730 .byte fallback lines analyzed, 49 unique mnemonics catalogued, 13 priorities identified. Top 3 targets (JR/JRL/CALR, displacement addressing, LDA) would bring native coverage from 41.7% to 75.1%.

title: MAME: Spurious button events during boot (voice dialog, transpose B)
notes: Running 'make fsanches_test' outside the VM, after boot sequence completes, some actions happen without user input: a dialog for selecting an instrument voice appears, and the screen shows transposition set to B (half step below default C). This suggests spurious button press events (possibly a 'transpose -' event). The control panel HLE or serial protocol may be generating ghost events. Needs investigation in MAME driver or control panel emulation.
close_reason: Verified fixed. Boot test with both existing and fresh NVRAM shows no spurious button events — no voice dialog, no unexpected transpose. Fix was m_steady_state flag (commit 8055121) plus prior protocol improvements.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: STATUS (Feb 21): MAME control panel emulation has seen significant work — serial TX/RX timing, self-clocking, button packet encoding, idle detection, and INTA mechanism fixes (visible in MAME and custom-roms commit histories). The spurious button events may be related to the serial protocol improvements that were still in progress. Needs retesting with current MAME build to see if the ghost events persist.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Note: While this issue remains open for MAME investigation, the Mines homebrew game now uses an 'input hijacking' trick by clearing the firmware's application event queue (0x02F838/0x02F83A) in each frame. This successfully suppresses all firmware-level actions for physical button presses while the game is running, including any spurious events that might occur during gameplay.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Investigating spurious button events. Will check current MAME control panel emulation code.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
ANALYSIS: Thorough code review of kn5000_cpanel HLE (941 lines) and firmware cpanel_routines.s boot sequence. Found real bug: HLE was suppressing ALL 0x20/0xE0 responses with non-zero params, but firmware uses 0x20 0x10 during boot in CPanel_ReadAllButtons and CPanel_InitButtonState. Fixed by adding m_steady_state flag (set on first phantom byte tx_start(0)). During boot, 0x20/0xE0 now responds with button data. After phantom bytes begin (steady-state), suppressed as before.

Additional analysis: the extensive protocol improvements (phantom byte suppression, PFFC deferred flags, self-clocking INTA, debounced button scan with 2-scan confirmation) added since the bug was reported (Feb 21) may have already fixed the root cause. The voice dialog and transpose B symptoms could also be NVRAM-related (persisted state from previous MAME session).

RELEASED: Fix committed (8055121d6c7) and pushed. Bug needs retesting with current MAME build to verify if the spurious events persist.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
RETEST (Mar 7): Tested MAME boot with both existing and fresh NVRAM. Results:
- Existing NVRAM: Clean main screen (PMEM 1-, Piano/Bigband Brass/Modern E.P.1, tempo 120). No voice dialog, no transpose change. Perfect.
- Fresh NVRAM: Shows 'Sound Name Error' (expected with empty Custom Data) and <G> key (corrupt settings default). No voice dialog popup, no spurious button events.

Original symptoms (voice selection dialog appearing, transpose set to B) are NOT reproducible. The m_steady_state fix (commit 8055121) combined with extensive protocol improvements (phantom bytes, PFFC, debounced scan) have resolved the issue.

title: MAME: Implement VRAM A18 banking (VGA display modes)
description: The VGA.A18 signal from the main CPU through a T7W139F decoder is not emulated (marked TODO at line 927 of kn5000.cpp). This controls VRAM bank selection, potentially affecting full-screen graphics, palette switching, and extended display modes. The display currently works for the main UI but some rendering modes (e.g., Feature Demo FTBMP bitmaps, full-screen splash screens) may depend on correct A18 banking. Need to: (1) trace the T7W139F decoder logic from the service manual schematic, (2) identify which firmware writes control the banking, (3) implement the bank switching in the VGA read/write handlers.
close_reason: Research complete: firmware never toggles A18 — uses only 76800 bytes (320x240 frame) of 256KB VRAM window. NOT blocking any functionality. Banking hardware exists (T7W139F decoder) but is unused in normal operation.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: RESEARCH COMPLETE (Mar 14): A18 banking is NOT blocking any known functionality. Firmware uses only 76800 bytes of VRAM (320x240 frame), never changes CRTC start address or toggles A18. No port writes found that control the T7W139F decoder. FTBMP rendering failure has a different root cause (SSF event routing — event 0x1C00006 never sent). Implementation plan documented but deferred as low-priority. Needs service manual schematics to confirm T7W139F wiring.

title: LLVM parallel build: Phase 2 (.byte fallback) complete
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Phase 2 of the LLVM parallel build is complete. The ASL-to-LLVM converter (scripts/asl_to_llvm.py) now produces a 100% byte-identical 2MB ROM using the LLVM toolchain (llvm-mc -> ld.lld -> llvm-objcopy). All CPU instructions are emitted as .byte sequences from the original ROM with ASL syntax preserved as comments. Key implementation: TLCS-900 instruction length decoder, address tracking with self-correction at labels, macro definition freeze (prevents phantom ADDR_TRACKER drift), and multi-instruction macro expansion sizing. Baseline: 131,076 .byte fallback lines to be progressively replaced with native LLVM instructions in Phase 3.

title: LLVM converter: Replace extpfx with semantic register-indirect instructions (~12,000)
description: Convert extpfx pseudo-instructions that use C0-C7/D0-D7/E0-E7/F0-F5 prefix bytes into semantic native instructions with proper register names and mnemonics. Currently 13,038 extpfx lines emit correct bytes but have no semantic meaning — the assembler doesn't understand what instruction they represent. The converter needs to decode the prefix byte to identify the base register and addressing mode, then the sub-opcode to identify the operation (LD, CP, ADD, etc.), and emit a proper native LLVM instruction. Top operations inside extpfx: LD (2,587), JR (1,141), EXTZ (1,025), CALL (566), CP (496), CALR (372), PUSH/POP (~700), LDA (324), ADD (280).
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: COMPLETED: This issue is now done. The ExtAddrMode LLVM backend format + Tier 43 categorization converts 12,788 previously-generic extpfx into semantic categories (erp/sri/spi/sd8/sd16/sd24/spd/dri/dpi/dd8/dd16/dd24/dpd + memory prefix mri/mrd). Only 250 extpfx remain (rare register prefix sub-opcodes). Done as part of kn5000-4rk9 and kn5000-qgz3.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: STATUS (Feb 21): Mines game builds and produces ROM output successfully. The game board renders on screen but with garbled graphics. The display subsystem is now well-understood (320x240 8bpp linear framebuffer, palette via 0x1703C8/C9), so the issue is likely in the tile/sprite rendering code in video.c, not in the display driver. May also be related to LLVM bug #10 (register swap on inlining) affecting tile_vram_ptr().

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: RESOLVED: Garbled graphics fixed. Root cause was LLVM backend encoding bugs (extended addressing modes), not tile rendering code. The same LLVM fixes that resolved kn5000-3z6 (button activation) also fixed the graphics. Playfield now renders correctly. Committed as part of 78d023c.

title: Audio: Analyze DSP effects processing algorithms
notes:
DSP effects processing is critical for audio emulation.

**Current state:** Dual DSP architecture documented, but register meanings and effect algorithms unknown.

**Required work:**
- Trace DSP register writes at 0x130000
- Document effect parameter mapping
- Analyze reverb, chorus, delay implementations
- Map effect chain configuration

**Phase:** 1 - Foundation (MAME Blockers)
**Blocks:** Audio synthesis in MAME
**Dependencies:** Audio hardware documentation
**Related:** kn5000-xv2 (DSP IC311), kn5000-si0 (effects chain)
close_reason: DSP effects processing fully analyzed: dual-chip architecture, port I/O protocol, bytecode interpreter (opcodes 0x0-0xF), all 6 native handlers decoded (1613 bytes), coefficient routing pipeline, parameter-to-DSP translation tables, effect-to-algorithm mapping (40 effects → 12 algo types), DSP internal address map, boot trace analysis. Remaining register-level semantics require dynamic MAME tracing which is tracked separately.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Starting DSP effects processing analysis. Will disassemble any .byte regions in DSP-related code first.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Progress: Disassembled 1594 bytes of DSP code in subcpu. DSP_ConfigBlock_Opaque → 5 functions (DSP_WriteAllChannelRegs, DSP_WriteChannelRegs_Inner, DSP_BlockCopyWords, DSP_FillMemWords, DSP_ChecksumRange) + data table. DSP_MixSendConfig_ExtData → 7 functions (DSP_RouteCoeffs_TypeA/B, DSP_CopyCoeffs_TypeA/B, DSP_VoiceCoeffRoute/Route2, DSP_AlgoCoeffLookup). Documented coefficient routing subsystem with memory layout, routing algorithm, register write protocol. 3 more DSP .byte blocks remain (Voice_DSP_InlineData 656B, DSP_VoiceParam_ExtData 155B, DSP_SetCoeff_ExtData 2088B).

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Progress: Disassembled ALL DSP .byte blocks in subcpu (4493 bytes total → 31 labeled functions). Blocks converted: DSP_ConfigBlock_Opaque (199B → 5 funcs), DSP_MixSendConfig_ExtData (1395B → 7 funcs), Voice_DSP_InlineData (656B → 4 funcs), DSP_VoiceParam_ExtData (155B → 1 func), DSP_SetCoeff_ExtData (2088B → 8 funcs), plus 6 utility functions. Documentation updated in audio-subsystem.md: DSP voice coefficient routing, register write protocol, coefficient setup pipeline, voice-to-DSP output configuration. Remaining work: deeper analysis of effect algorithms (reverb/chorus/delay specifics), DSP bytecode program decoding, register-level behavior documentation.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Progress: DSP bytecode interpreter fully documented. Architecture: bytecodes are 2-byte headers (opcode nibble + 12-bit data count) followed by data. Opcodes 0-5 dispatch to native TLCS-900 handlers within DSP_Bytecode_Programs (0x3C32E, ~1200 bytes of prevbank/auto-increment register write code). Opcode 0xE sends command+data directly to DSP hardware. Config entries are 12 bytes (4 words + program pointer). DSP_State_Dispatcher pipeline analyzed: 11-step conditional pipeline with state structure (+2=algo change, +4=mode, +6=algo number). Algorithm change loads 7 ROM config blocks with inter-block delay. Documentation committed to audio-subsystem.md. Remaining: decode the 6 native opcode handlers (0-5) and the actual DSP register semantics.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Progress: Decoded DSP command protocol from actual bytecode programs in ROM. Key findings: (1) DSP cmd 0x01 = bulk init (150-350 bytes), cmd 0x03 = algorithm select, cmd 0x30 = parameter write with sub-addressing. (2) Algorithm change sequence uses 8 programs: init → enable → flush → channel 0 coefficients → channel 1 coefficients with yield between large writes. (3) Coefficient block format: cmd 0x03 → OP1 header (8B) → OP2 coefficients (93B). (4) Reverb and chorus configs share common 28-byte DSP state header. (5) DSP accepts 12-bit parameter values for effects, 16-bit for initialization. Remaining: decode per-algorithm parameter tables at 0x1F09C/0x1F22C, map MainCPU parameter indices to DSP registers.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Progress: Decoded per-algorithm parameter pointer tables at 0x1F09C (register addresses) and 0x1F22C (parameter mapping). Found entries for algorithms 1-6 (chorus/modulation), 8-10 (gated/delay), 15-19 (reverb). Tables use 12-byte stride entries with bytecode program pointers. Static analysis has reached practical limits — register-level semantics will require dynamic tracing in MAME (watching actual DSP writes during effect changes). RELEASED: DSP analysis phase complete (protocol, bytecodes, tables fully documented). Remaining register semantics require MAME-based approach.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Starting MAME dynamic tracing approach for DSP register semantics.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Progress: Captured full DSP1 boot trace via MAME (-log). Identified 6-phase boot sequence with program module types (0x00=EFF header, 0x3C=init, 0x40/0x47=patches, 0x54=chorus, 0xC8=reverb). Default boot loads reverb on ch1 with 8 all-pass filters + 26 delay taps. Documented in audio-subsystem.md. MAME DSP device updated: VOICE DATA→DSP PROGRAM with module docs. Both committed+pushed. Next: trace effect changes via UI simulation or implement algo auto-detection from coefficient patterns.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Found effect-to-algo mapping table at SubCPU ROM 0x01F596. Complete mapping of 40 effects to 12 algo types documented. Groups: 2=modulation (chorus/flanger/phaser/etc), 3=delay/gate, 4=mod delay, 5-8=reverb variants, 9-10=distortion/overdrive/compressor, 11=dynamics. Also found routing config at 0x00F490 and parameter name pool at MainCPU 0xE324C4. Algo byte upper nibble encodes routing state. Documented in audio-subsystem.md. Next: trace DSP coefficient patterns for different algorithms, update MAME HLE.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Updated MAME DSP device: algo_to_category now uses correct algo types (2-11) instead of effect indices. Documented algo selection mechanism (CMD 0x01 program modules, not CMD 0x30). All committed and pushed.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Added DSP1 internal address map to docs. 29 unique coefficient addresses identified from boot trace. Reverb: 26-pair delay line array @0x00, 8 all-pass filter stages @0x1D-0x3D, dry/wet mix @0x06/@0x86, gain @0x90, tail filters @0x9E-0xB2. Chorus: LFO waveform table @0x00, delay params @0x26, LFO rates @0x09-0x0A, 4 all-pass stages @0x1D-0x29. Hypothesized parameter↔address mapping documented. Remaining: verify mapping by tracing parameter changes, capture coefficient data for other algo types.

title: Investigate Feature Demo (FTDEMO) display failure
description: The Internal Feature Presentation DEMO mode does not show FTBMP images on screen. It appears to get stuck before reaching the bitmap drawing code. Need to trace the code path from demo activation through floppy disk loading to bitmap display, identify the blockage, and fix or workaround it in MAME.
close_reason: Root cause confirmed: Feature Demo requires user button-press navigation to trigger FDB3D1 event buffer writer. Without input, LABEL_F98697 never fires post-boot. Table[1] logic is correct. No MAME bug — just no simulated input in automated tests.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Starting investigation of FTDEMO display failure.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
Investigation complete. Key findings:

1. FTBMP images are in Table Data ROM (not floppy disk) — loaded via file entry index at 0x8CE01C
2. The demo is driven by AcPresentationControlProc processing hkst_55.ssf XML from ROM
3. Audio_WaitForReady (polls 0x420 bit 2) has a 61440-iteration timeout — not a hard block
4. Most likely culprit: EV_READPRESENTATION/EV_READACTION events not being delivered correctly, stalling the presentation state machine
5. MAME floppy bug fixed: kn5000_floppies was '35dd' (720K), now '35hd' (1.44MB HD) — real KN5000 disks couldn't be mounted
6. Added trace script at /tmp/ftdemo_investigate.lua for user to run while manually navigating to demo
7. feature-demo.md updated with bitmap loading chain, MAME status, floppy type bug

RELEASED: Investigation done; active debugging requires user to navigate to demo in MAME.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
TRACE ANALYSIS (v1 script had wrong addresses):

Key findings from v1 trace reanalysis:
- 'AcFdemoScreenProc' tap at even(0xF84149)=0xF84148 was WRONG: it was firing during VwUserBitmapSpProc's ret epilog at F84148, NOT AcFdemoScreenProc. PC=F84145/F84146 confirmed this was the epilog.
- 'EventDispatch' tap at 0xFA9660: CORRECT — FA9660 = SendEvent function (confirmed via unidasm: 'lda XSP,XSP+0xE8'). Fires every frame because DispatchEvent's epilog runs at FA965C and MAME prefetch reads FA9660.
- 'InitializeEventQueue' at FA9584 = single ret byte (0x0E), consistent with disassembly.
- DemoModeFunc actual ROM address: 0xF222CC (found via binary search for call pattern to 0xF8696F/0xF869E3).

CORRECTED FUNCTION ADDRESSES:
- DemoModeFunc = 0xF222CC (was NOT being monitored in v1!)
- DemoMode_Main_Operation = 0xF8696F (tap at F8696E)
- DemoMode_Initialize = 0xF869E3 (tap at F869E2)
- AcFdemoScreenProc = 0xF84149 (tap at F84148, but distinguishable by PC >= F84149)
- SendEvent = 0xFA9660 (confirmed correct)
- DispatchEvent = 0xFA9585 (tap at FA9584)

New investigation script written: /tmp/ftdemo2.lua — uses correct addresses and logs XBC/XDE for key functions to see what event codes flow during demo activation.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
Progress 2026-02-28: Deep analysis of AcPresentationControlProc dispatch chain.

Key findings:
1. AcPresentCtrl IS being called (PC=F8450B) at frame 1103+ with real events
2. Receives 0x1C0001C with XDE=0x0001BFC8 (ALWAYS same address)
3. Handler at F84625 checks: *(0x0001BFC8) == 0xB80A before sending 0x1C00006 (SSF start)
4. If check fails, SSF never starts → no images ever appear
5. 0x0001BFC8 is valid DRAM (mapped 0x000000-0x0FFFFF in MAME driver)

Identified dispatch table structure:
- RegisterObjectTable copies 14 bytes to 0x027ED2 + type*14
- Types 0x000-0xFF default to BitmapHandler (0xFA5995)
- Type 0x001 handler: BitmapHandler (not DemoModeFunc!)
- Type 0x013 handler: overridden to DemoModeFunc (confirmed firing at frame 883 with XDE=0)

AcPresentCtrl jump table (events 0x1C00002-0x1C0000C):
- 0x1C00002 → F84563 (EVT_DISPLAY handler)
- 0x1C00006 → F84650 (starts SSF: clears counters, calls 0xFC5625)
- 0x1C00007-9 → F84580 (sends 0x1E00029 to 0x02600024)
- All others → F8468B/F84687 (exit)

The 0x1C00006 event starts SSF reading by calling 0xFC5625 with WA=8.
This event is NEVER received because 0x1C0001C handler check fails.

Next step: Run ftdemo4.lua to confirm B80A check passes/fails and see workspace content at 0x0001BFC8.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
Root cause confirmed (Feb 2026 deep analysis):

The Feature Demo image display failure has a confirmed root cause in the event routing system. Full findings:

1. AcPresentCtrl_CheckSSFStart (0xF84625) checks *(XDE) == 0x0000B80A when handling event 0x1C0001C. SSF only starts if this passes.

2. TWO code paths send event 0x1C0001C:
   - DemoMenu_BuildItemWorkspace (0xF83CEA): QUEUED via FA9D58. Workspace tag = table[iz]+R*1024, always in 0x82xx range. NEVER produces 0xB80A. This is what fires in MAME — and always fails the check.
   - GroupBoxProc_StartSSFPresentation (0xF9A273): DIRECT via FA9660. Builds workspace byte-by-byte from stack params, producing tag 0x0000B80A. This is the CORRECT path, but it NEVER fires in MAME.

3. GroupBoxProc_StartSSFPresentation is reached when GroupBoxProc receives event 0x1C00038 (or 0x1C00030). These events are never routed to GroupBoxProc during Feature Demo navigation in MAME.

4. Remaining question: what should send 0x1C00038 to GroupBoxProc, and why doesn't it happen in MAME?

Actions taken:
- Added semantic labels and function headers to kn5000_v10_program.s:
  - LABEL_F83CEA → DemoMenu_BuildItemWorkspace (with full header doc)
  - LABEL_F83D8D → DemoMenu_BuildItemWorkspace_Post
  - LABEL_F84625 → AcPresentCtrl_CheckSSFStart (with investigation notes)
  - LABEL_F9A21E → GroupBoxProc_Ev1C00030
  - LABEL_F9A273 → GroupBoxProc_StartSSFPresentation (with critical path doc)
  - LABEL_F9A2BA → GroupBoxProc_SSFItemLoop
  - GroupBoxProc: added full function header
- Updated feature-demo.md with confirmed root cause, workspace concept, call-chain overview, and next steps
- All 6 ROMs: 100% byte-match maintained

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Resuming GroupBoxProc investigation. New findings: GroupBoxProc at 0xF99840, broadcast events resolve to active widget at DRAM[0x02F83C] not all widgets. Trace script ftdemo6.lua prepared.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Tracing what should send event 0x1C00038 to GroupBoxProc.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
Deep trace of 0x1C00038 event chain (Mar 1 2026):

Identified the full chain of code that should send event 0x1C00038 to GroupBoxProc:

1. LABEL_F98697 (0xF98697) -- THE SENDER
   - Embedded in handler chain tables (LABEL_EE7FA8 etc.) as a function pointer
   - Fires when widgets using those chains receive user-interaction events
   - Reads DRAM 0x8D38 as index R; looks up ROM array at E01F80+R*4
   - Walks ROM array comparing state bytes (0xC07D/0xC080) to find a match
   - On match (or 0xFFFE unconditional): pushes event 0x1C00038 to FA9945

2. FA9945 -- QUEUE PROCESSOR/ROUTER
   - Processes circular event queue at DRAM 0x02BC34 (PostEvent=FA9752 writes to it)
   - For queued events with code 0x1C00038: checks upper16 of param vs. stored value
   - If queue empty: posts directly via FA9D58

3. FA9752 = PostEvent -- EVENT QUEUE WRITER
   - Inserts 12-byte entries (self, event_code, param) into circular buffer

4. HYPOTHESIS: F98697 never fires in MAME because either:
   a) No widget with the EE7FA8/EE7FD4/EE7FFC handler chains is active during demo
   b) DRAM 0x8D38 selector points to an array whose entries don't match current state
   c) State bytes (0xC07D/0xC080) never match the ROM array entry 0x9100

Actions taken:
- Added function header to LABEL_F98697 block explaining full logic
- Updated feature-demo.md with detailed event chain analysis (FA9945, FA9752/PostEvent)
- Wrote Lua verification script at /tmp/ftdemo_v6.lua:
  Monitors FA9752 (PostEvent) calls, F98697 calls, FA9945 with XBC=0x1C00038,
  GroupBoxProc_StartSSFPresentation entry, and AcPresentCtrl B80A checks
  with state snapshot (0x8D38, 0x8D3A, 0xC07D-0xC080, routing table contents)

RELEASED: investigation complete pending Lua trace run in MAME.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Updating with MAME Lua trace results - final root cause analysis

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
Root cause confirmed via MAME Lua trace (March 2026):

LABEL_F98697 is called ~900 times during boot init (8D38=0x00, table[0]={0xFFFF} sentinel → always returns early, no event sent). After boot stabilizes (8D38=0x01), FDB3D1 (event buffer writer at 0xBD3C) is never called again because no user input occurs in MAME's automated test → event buffer stays empty → F98697 never fires post-boot.

Table entry[1] DOES contain 0x7002 at ROM 0xE0156E — F98697 WOULD send event 0x1C00038 if called with chain C080=0x70, param C07D=0x02, which is exactly what user navigation would produce on real hardware.

No MAME emulation bug. The Feature Demo requires actual user button-press navigation to trigger the dispatcher. Automated MAME tests don't simulate this.

Updating feature-demo.md with full findings. RELEASED: investigation complete.

title: LLVM converter: Native LD with memory operands (~40,000+ instructions)
description:
## Goal
Convert the massive LD instruction backlog from .byte fallback to native LLVM. This is the single largest category (~53,807 fallbacks) and covers many sub-forms.

## Sub-form breakdown

### Direct memory loads: LD reg, (nn) — ~7,248 instances
- Extended addressing: C1/D1/E1 prefix + 16-bit addr + LD sub-opcode
- 24-bit addressing: C2/D2/E2 prefix + 24-bit addr + LD sub-opcode
- Example: .byte 0xc1, 0x02, 0x04, 0x21 → ld a, (0x0402)

### Direct memory stores: LD (nn), reg — ~6,411 instances
- Extended addressing: F0/F1 prefix + addr + store sub-opcode + reg_or_imm
- 24-bit addressing: F2 prefix + 24-bit addr + store sub-opcode
- Example: .byte 0xf1, 0x09, 0x04, 0x60 → ld (0x0409), xwa

### Direct memory store immediate: LD (nn), #imm — ~1,698 instances (opcode 0x08)
- 3-byte: 0x08, addr_lo, imm8 (8-bit I/O register writes)
- Example: .byte 0x08, 0x3c, 0x00 → ld (0x3C), 0x00

### Register-indirect with displacement: LD reg, (Xreg+d8/d16) — ~167+349 instances
- d8 form: prefix_base+8+r, d8, sub-opcode (3 bytes)
- d16 form: F3, mode_byte, d16, sub-opcode (5+ bytes)
- Example: .byte 0x9f, 0x04, 0x23 → ld hl, (xsp+4)

### Prefix LD r,#imm (prefix + 0x03 + imm) — ~33 instances
- 8-bit: C8+r, 0x03, imm8 (3 bytes)
- 16-bit: D8+r, 0x03, imm16 (4 bytes)
- Already supported as PrefixLDImm format in MCCodeEmitter

### Other LD forms
- LD r8,#imm8 (0x20-0x27) — 5,533 (separate issue kn5000-aq9)
- LD r16,#imm16 (0x30-0x37) — 6,301 (separate issue kn5000-aq9)
- LD r32,#imm32 (0x40-0x47) — already native (Tier 2)
- LD r,r (2-byte) — already native (Tier 3)
- LD r,(Xreg) / LD (Xreg),r (2-byte) — already native (Tier 7)

## LLVM backend status
- MemLoad format: reg_prefix + (opcode + dst_reg) — supported
- MemStore format: dst_prefix + (opcode + src_reg_or_imm) — supported
- MemLoadDst (LDA): dst_prefix + (opcode + dst_reg) — supported
- Direct addressing via F2/E2: 24-bit address with fixup — supported
- Register-indirect with d8/d16 displacement: emitMemPrefix handles all cases

## Implementation strategy
Recommend implementing in phases due to complexity:
1. **Phase A:** Direct memory with extended addressing (C1-E2/F0-F2 prefixes) — highest count
2. **Phase B:** Register-indirect with d8 displacement — moderate count
3. **Phase C:** LD (nn),#imm8 (0x08 opcode) — 1,698 instances
4. **Phase D:** Prefix LD r,#imm forms — small count

### Key challenge: Address operand syntax
LLVM syntax for memory operands: "ld a, (xhl)" vs "ld a, (xhl, 4)" for displacement.
Must match exactly what the LLVM AsmParser expects. Check TLCS900AsmParser.cpp or .td files.

For direct addressing with symbols/labels, can use either:
- Numeric: ld a, (0x0402, 0) — base=0x0402, disp=0
- Symbolic: ld a, (SYMBOL, 0) — if the address has a known label

## Verification
1. Regenerate, build, compare_roms → 100.00%
2. Spot-check: count native ld instructions vs remaining .byte LD fallbacks
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Converted register-indirect LD loads (Tier 18), LD stores, and LDA (Tier 19) to native. 26,437 new native instructions. Fallback: 58,351. d8=0 cases skipped (LLVM optimizes to shorter encoding). Direct addressing (F2/E2 prefix) LD not yet done.

title: FeatureDemo: Refactor assembly to use slide macros
description: Replace raw data definitions in assembly source with new macros. Convert existing db/dw sequences to SLIDE_BEGIN, WIDGET_*, SLIDE_END. Verify rebuilt ROM still matches original byte-for-byte. Measure source code line reduction.

title: Boot: Document reset vector and early initialization
description: Trace main CPU behavior from reset vector (0xE00000 area). Document: initial stack pointer setup, memory controller configuration, clock/PLL initialization, watchdog setup. Identify the entry point and first instructions executed after power-on.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
Main CPU Reset Handler Analysis (from kn5000_v10_program.asm):

**Reset Vector:** 0xFFFF00 points to RESET_HANDLER at 0xEF03C6

**Early Initialization Sequence (0xEF03C6):**

1. **Watchdog Timer Disable:**
   - WDMOD = 0x00 (disable watchdog)
   - WDCR = 0xB1 (watchdog control)

2. **Clock Configuration:**
   - CLKMOD = 0x04 (clock mode select)

3. **Port Configuration (I/O Setup):**
   - PF/PFFC/PFCR: Control panel enabled, MIDI disabled (PFFC=0x73)
   - P2FC/P3FC = 0xFF (full function on ports 2/3)
   - P7/P7FC/P7CR, PA/PAFC, PB/PBFC, PC/PCFC/PCCR
   - PD/PDFC/PDCR, PE/PEFC/PECR, PH/PHFC/PHCR
   - PZ/PZCR

4. **Timer Initialization:**
   - T01MOD = 0x1D, T23MOD = 0x1D
   - T02FFCR = 0x00, TREG0 = 0x0A, TREG1 = 0x10
   - T8RUN bit 1 set
   - T4MOD = 0x05, T4FFCR = 0x00, T16CR = 0x00
   - TREG4L = 0x0001, TREG5L = 0x3D09
   - T16RUN bits 7 and 0 set

5. **Memory Controller Setup:**
   - MSAR0-5: Memory segment start addresses (0x1E, 0x10, 0xC0, 0x00, 0x80, 0x00)
   - MAMR0-5: Memory segment masks (0x0F, 0x3F, 0x7F, 0x1F, 0xFF, 0xFF)
   - P8/P8FC/P8CR configuration

6. **DRAM Initialization (0xEF04A1-0xEF04BC):**
   - Short pause loop (BC = 0x400 iterations)
   - DRAM1REF = 0x81
   - Longer pause loop (BC = 0x2000 iterations)
   - DRAM1REF = 0x71
   - DRAM1CRL = 0x8B, DRAM1CRH = 0x58
   - PMEMCR bit 4 cleared

7. **Bus Chip Select Setup:**
   - B0CSL-B5CSL, B0CSH-B5CSH (bus control registers for external devices)

8. **Serial & DAC:**
   - SC0MOD = 0x29, SC0CR = 0x00 (serial channel 0)
   - DAREG0/1 = 0xFF, DADRV = 0x03 (DAC output)

9. **Stack Pointer Initialization (0xEF0526):**
   - XSP = 0x00000C00 (stack at top of internal RAM)

**Post-Init Sequence:**
- Calls Seems_to_copy_some_data_buffers
- Calls MainCPU_self_test_routines
- Calls Get_Firmware_Version
- If running boot ROM (version 0xFF): displays "Please Wait !!" bitmap
- Initializes system timestamp to 0
- Calls peripheral init routines
- Sets interrupt priorities (INTET01, INTET45)
- Detects area/region code
- Checks for HD-AE5000 expansion board (PE bit 0)
- Reads control panel buttons for update mode check
- Optional: FLASH_MEM_UPDATE if holding specific button combo

Status: Issue can be closed - reset vector and early init fully documented.

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text: REOPENING: Website documentation not updated. Next steps: Update reverse-engineering.md 'Reset Vector and Early Init' section (lines ~507-515) with actual findings from comments. Replace generic 'Tasks:' list with documented reset handler address (0xEF03C6), watchdog disable sequence, clock config, port setup, timer init, memory controller, DRAM init, serial/DAC setup, stack pointer. Add to memory-map.md any new register addresses discovered.

title: LLVM converter: Split chunked multi-instruction .byte blocks into individual instructions (~15,700 instructions)
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: DESCRIPTION: The converter currently dumps entire subroutines as raw 8-byte-per-line .byte blocks when it encounters instructions it can't handle. 6,856 lines (54,848 bytes, ~15,700 instructions) are affected. Fix: instead of giving up on the whole block, split the packed bytes into individual instructions using known instruction lengths from ASL output, then convert each one separately. Most individual instructions within these blocks ARE convertible — the block was only skipped because one or two instructions in it weren't.

title: LLVM converter: PUSH imm16 mnemonic mismatch (51 fallbacks)
description: Tier 23 checks mnem_upper == 'PUSHW' but ASL source uses 'PUSH'. Fix: also accept 'PUSH' in the condition. Affects table_data (51 instances). Opcode 0x0B + imm16_LE = 3 bytes.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: FIXED: Changed Tier 23 to accept 'PUSH' in addition to 'PUSHW'. Actual fallback was 1 (not 51 — the rest were db-directive bytes in ASL source).

title: Symbols: Rename AudioMix to a more accurate name
notes: AudioMix appears to be a misnomer. Need to investigate what the symbol actually does and choose a better semantic name. Apply sed-based renaming across all assembly files and documentation per policy.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: INVESTIGATION COMPLETE (Feb 21): Examined the hardware at 0x150000 and the AudioMix_* routines. The device is a register-indirect audio mixer/attenuator with 32 channels organized in 4 groups of 8. Register map: data regs at (group*0x20+0x10) through (group*0x20+0x17), enable regs at 0x1F/0x3F/0x5F/0x7F. Init writes 0x5A to all channel levels. The 4 groups likely correspond to 4 tone generator ICs, each handling 8 voices. The table_data bootcode at 0xFFFA5C also accesses this device with the same register structure. CONCLUSION: 'AudioMix' is an accurate name for an audio mixer/attenuator device. The MAME driver already describes it as 'Audio mixer/attenuator at 0x150000'. No rename needed — closing as investigated, name is correct.

title: LLVM converter: Native LDA (load effective address) (~11,565 instructions)
description:
## Goal
Convert LDA (Load Effective Address) from .byte fallback to native LLVM.

## Instruction forms

### LDA Xreg, (nn) — 3-byte form (~6,071 instances)
- Prefix: B0+r (B0 table) or F0 for extended
- Sub-opcode: 0x30+r (LDA in B0 table)
- Example: .byte 0xf0, 0xe4, 0x31 → lda xbc, (0xE4) [I/O register INTET01]

### LDA Xreg, nn:24 — 5-byte form (~3,579 instances)
- Prefix: F2 (24-bit direct addressing)
- 24-bit address + sub-opcode 0x30+r
- Example: .byte 0xf2, 0x00, 0xb0, 0x0a, 0x31 → lda xbc, (0x0AB000)

### LDA Xreg, (nn:16) — 4-byte form (~1,603 instances)
- Prefix: F0/F1 (extended 8/16-bit addressing)
- Example: .byte 0xf0, 0x80, 0x30 → lda xwa, (0x80)

### LDA Xreg, (Xreg+d) — 2-byte form (~311 instances)  
- Register-indirect with LDA sub-opcode in B0 table
- Example: .byte 0xb0, 0x30 → lda xwa, (xwa)

## LLVM backend status
- MemLoadDst format: Already supported — uses dst_mem_prefix + (opcode + dst_reg)
- LDA is in the B0 opcode table (destination-memory prefix)
- Direct addressing via F2 prefix: supported
- MCCodeEmitter encodes this correctly

## Converter changes needed
File: scripts/asl_to_llvm.py, in try_convert_native()

### Encoding detection
LDA uses the B0 sub-opcode table. The sub-opcode for LDA is 0x30+r where r is the 32-bit destination register index:
- r=0: XWA, r=1: XBC, r=2: XDE, r=3: XHL, r=4: XIX, r=5: XIY, r=6: XIZ, r=7: XSP

### Address prefix types
- B0+r: register-indirect (Xreg) — no displacement
- B8+r, d8: register-indirect with 8-bit displacement
- F0, addr8: 8-bit I/O register address
- F1, addr16_LE: 16-bit address  
- F2, addr24_LE: 24-bit address
- F3, mode, d16: register-indirect with 16-bit displacement

### Implementation approach
1. Identify the prefix type from rom_bytes[0]
2. Decode the address/displacement
3. Find the LDA sub-opcode (0x30-0x37) to get destination register
4. Emit native: lda <xreg>, (<address_expr>)

### Key challenge
Must match LLVM's expected syntax for LDA operands. The MemLoadDst format expects (base_reg, disp) or (symbol_expr, 0) syntax. Check .td files or test with llvm-mc.

## Verification
1. Regenerate, build, compare_roms → 100.00%
2. Spot-check: grep -cP '^\s+lda ' in output
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
Serial routine flag usage (from docs/cp_serial_flags.txt):

**Flag CP_Flags_A (0x8D8C):**
- Bit 2: Set by LABEL_FC490E/FC4915 (appears unused - nothing reads it)
- Bit 4: Never set, but checked by LABEL_FC4A40
- Bit 6: Set by SOME_CPANEL_ROUTINE__FC3EF5
- Bit 10: Used by multiple routines for handshaking

**Flag CP_Flags_B (0x8D92):**
- Bit 0: Primary handshake flag, used by most send routines
- Bit 1: Set by CPANEL_SERIAL_ROUTINE_1 (unused?)
- Bit 2: Set by CPANEL_SERIAL_ROUTINE_0 (unused?)
- Bit 3: Set by LABEL_FC4B10 (unused?)
- Bit 6: Set by INTA_HANDLER (unused?)
- Bit 7: Checked by ready-to-send routine

**Flag CP_Flags_C (0x8D93):**
- Bits 0,4: Initial communication test results (set by LABEL_FC4194)

**Key routines identified:**
- CHECK_IF_WE_ARE_READY_TO_SEND_CMD_TO_CPANEL: Uses A.10, B.7
- SEND_CMD_TO_CPANEL: Uses A.10
- INTA_HANDLER: Uses A.10, B.6
- CPANEL_SERIAL_ROUTINE_0: Uses A.10, B.2, B.7
- CPANEL_SERIAL_ROUTINE_1: Uses A.10, B.1
- CPANEL_SERIAL_ROUTINE_6: Uses A.10
- CPANEL_SERIAL_ROUTINE_7: Uses B.0
- CPANEL_SERIAL_ROUTINE_8: Uses A.10, B.0

title: Update: Map file types to system components
description: Document which file types update which system components: main CPU program ROM, sub CPU payload, table data, custom data flash, HDAE5000 firmware. Create matrix showing file extension/name pattern vs target component.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
**File Type to System Component Mapping:**

| File Type ID | Header String | Target Component | Memory Address |
|--------------|---------------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Program DATA FILE 1/2 | Main CPU ROM (first half) | 0xE00000+ |
| 2 | Program DATA FILE 2/2 | Main CPU ROM (second half) | 0xF00000+ |
| 3 | Table DATA FILE 1/2 | Table Data ROM (first half) | 0x800000+ |
| 4 | Table DATA FILE 2/2 | Table Data ROM (second half) | 0x900000+ |
| 5 | CMPCUSTOMDATA FILE | Custom Data Flash | 0x300000 |
| 6 | HD-AEPRG DATA FILE | HDAE5000 ROM | 0x280000 |
| 7 | Program DATA FILE PCK | Main CPU ROM (packed) | 0xE00000 |
| 8 | Table DATA FILE PCK | Table Data ROM (packed) | 0x800000 |

**Component Sizes:**
| Component | Size | File Split |
|-----------|------|------------|
| Main CPU ROM | 2 MB | 2 disks (1/2, 2/2) or 1 packed |
| Table Data ROM | 2 MB | 2 disks (1/2, 2/2) or 1 packed |
| Custom Data | 1 MB | 1 disk (compressed?) |
| HDAE5000 ROM | 512 KB | 1 disk |

**Notes:**
- Sub CPU payload (192KB) is embedded within main CPU ROM, not a separate update file
- PCK files are likely packed/compressed single-disk versions
- Split files must be loaded in order (1/2 then 2/2)
- Handler at SHOW_ILLEGAL_DISK_MESSAGE shown if 2/2 loaded before 1/2

Status: Mapping complete from firmware analysis.

title: Plan: Long-term project roadmap and phase tracking
notes:
Master roadmap for KN5000 reverse engineering project.

## Project Goals
1. **100% ROM Reconstruction** - Byte-accurate rebuilds of all firmware ✅ COMPLETE
2. **MAME Emulation** - Full system emulation for preservation (IN PROGRESS)
3. **Homebrew Development** - Enable custom software creation (App Loader WORKING)
4. **Documentation** - Complete technical reference ✅ COMPLETE

## Phase Structure with Tracking Issues

### Phase 1: Foundation (MAME Blockers) - kn5000-dbi ✅ CLOSED
*Goal: Get basic emulator running with display and sound*
All Phase 1 sub-issues complete. MAME boots with display and audio subsystem traffic logged.

### Phase 2: Core Functionality - kn5000-dnl (OPEN)
*Goal: User interaction and file I/O working*

**UI/Input (kn5000-1vz): ✅ CLOSED**
- All sub-issues complete. UI navigation verified working in MAME.

**Storage (kn5000-a0k): IN PROGRESS**
- FDC fully wired (UPD72067, MSR at 0x110008, FIFO at 0x11000A, DMA at 0x120000)
- HDAE5000 IDE/ATA working — App Loader reads FAT16 from hard disk
- Custom Data Flash: mapped, NVRAM-backed
- Table Data ROM: working (sequencer reads rhythm data)
- Remaining: floppy disk read test (floppy images ready, FDC wired)

### Phase 3: Complete Documentation - kn5000-9m6 ✅ CLOSED
*Goal: All subsystems fully documented*
All subsystem pages documented, no placeholders remain.

### Phase 4: Quality & Polish - kn5000-nca (OPEN)
*Goal: Production-ready emulation and homebrew support*
- Validation test suite: created (boot/menu/display tests)
- SDK documentation: comprehensive (1442 lines, Quick Start guide, Makefile template)

## Recent Milestones (Mar 2026)
- **Tone Generator device (IC303)** — kn5000_tonegen_device added to MAME. 64-voice PCM wavetable with register-indirect interface, waveform ROM reading, stereo 48kHz output. First step toward actual sound.
- **UART mode** — 8-bit UART TX/RX implemented in TMP94C241 serial. Enables MIDI output at 31250 baud.
- **MIDI output** — TX0 wired to midi_port device. MAME emits MIDI.
- **FDC address mapping** — UPD72067 registers properly mapped with PC AT layout + 16-bit bus doubling.
- **R+d16 LLVM addressing** — SRI prefix encoding fixed, 357 .byte→native conversions in roms-disasm.
- **App Loader (HDAE5000)** — Working end-to-end: FAT16 filesystem, menu UI, APP.BIN loading, Mines game launches from disk.
- **ROM reconstruction** — 279,798 native instructions, 0 .byte fallbacks, 100% byte match on all 6 ROMs.

title: ASL Macros: Document new TMP94C241 instruction encodings
description:
Several new macros were added to tmp94c241.inc during subcpu_boot disassembly:

Jump/Call macros:
- JRL_T target: Jump relative long (78 LL HH) - 3 bytes vs jp's 4 bytes
- CALR target: Call relative (1e LL HH) - 3 bytes vs call's 4 bytes  
- CALL_ABS24 target: Call absolute 24-bit (1d LL MM HH)

Block transfer macros:
- LDIR_94: Block copy with TMP94C241 encoding (83 11 vs ASL's 85 11)

Register load macros (for correct immediate encoding):
- LD_A value: Load A with immediate (21 nn)
- LD_D value: Load D with immediate (24 nn)

These address encoding differences between TMP94C241 and TMP96C141 (which ASL targets).

TODO: Document general encoding patterns for creating future macros.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
Additional macros added during DMA routine disassembly:

Stack Frame Operations:
- DEC_6_XSP: Allocate 6 bytes on stack (ef 6e)
- INC_6_XSP: Deallocate 6 bytes from stack (ef 66)
- INC_0_XSP: Adjust stack by 0 bytes (ef 60) - used for alignment

IZ Register Operations:
- LD_IZ_BC: Load IZ from BC (d9 8e)
- CP_IZ_imm16 value: Compare IZ with 16-bit immediate (de cf LL HH)
- SUB_IZ_imm16 value: Subtract 16-bit immediate from IZ
- LD_C_IZL: Load C from low byte of IZ

Zero-Extend Operations:
- EXTZ_WA: Zero-extend A to WA
- EXTZ_BC: Zero-extend C to BC

DMA Register Operations:
- LDC_DMAS2_XDE/XWA/XBC/XHL: Set DMA channel 2 source
- LDC_DMAD2_XWA, LDC_DMAD0_XWA/XBC: Set DMA destination
- LDC_DMAC2_BC/WA/A, LDC_DMAC0_WA/A: Set DMA count

Stack-Relative Operations:
- LD_pXSP_d_XDE d: Store XDE at (XSP+d)
- LD_XDE_pXSP_d d: Load XDE from (XSP+d)
- LD_A_pXSP_d d: Load A from (XSP+d)
- ADD_pXSP_d_XWA d: Add XWA to (XSP+d)

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
Complete macro inventory from tmp94c241.inc (135 macros total):

**DMA Control Macros (21 macros):**
- Source registers: LDC_DMAS0_XWA, LDC_DMAS2_XWA/XBC/XDE/XHL, LDC_DMAS3_XHL
- Destination: LDC_DMAD0_XWA/XBC, LDC_DMAD2_XWA, LDC_DMAD3_XHL
- Count: LDC_DMAC0_A/WA, LDC_DMAC2_A/BC/WA/XWA, LDC_DMAC3_A
- Mode: LDC_DMAM0_WA, LDC_DMAM2_WA/BC, LDC_DMAM3_BC, LDC_WA_DMAM0
- Misc: LDC_INTNEST_WA

**8-bit Register Load Macros (8 macros):**
LD_W, LD_A, LD_B, LD_C, LD_D, LD_E, LD_H, LD_L

**Memory Transfer Macros (12 macros):**
- Block transfers: LDI, LDIR, LDIR_83, LDIW, LDIRW_93, LDIRW_95, LDDR_85
- Memory-to-memory: LDW_16_16, LD_8_8
- Address load: LDA variants (19 different encodings for auto-increment modes)

**Multiplication Macros (20 macros):**
- Unsigned 8-bit: MUL_A/C/E/L
- Unsigned 16-bit: MUL_WA/BC/DE/HL/IX/IY/IZ, MULW_WA
- Signed: MULS_A/C/L/WA/BC/DE/HL/IX/IY/IZ/IYL
- Register pairs: MULS_XWA_IX/DE, MULS_XDE_IY

**Division Macros (14 macros):**
- Unsigned 8-bit: DIV_A/C/E/L
- Word: DIVW_WA/BC/DE/HL/IX/IY/IZ
- Signed word: DIVS_WA/BC/DE/HL/IX/IY

**Shift/Rotate Macros (19 macros):**
SRA_0/8_XWA/XBC/XDE, SLA_0/2/8_XWA/XBC/XDE/XHL/XIX, SRL_0_XWA/XBC/XDE/XHL, SLL_0/8_XWA/XBC/XDE/XHL/XIX

**Sub CPU Boot ROM Macros (added during disassembly):**
- Stack: INC_0_XBC, PUSH_WORD, DEC_6_XSP, INC_6_XSP, INC_0_XSP
- Compare: CP_pXWA_WORD, CP_pXBC_d_WORD, CP_IZ_imm16, CP_MEM24_IMM16
- Load address: LDA_XWA_IMM24, LDA_XDE_IMM24
- Memory store: LD_MEM24_IMM16, LD_pXIX_IMM16, LD_pXHL_IMM16
- Jump/Call: CALR, CALL_ABS24, JRL_T
- Block: LDIR_94 (TMP94C241 encoding vs ASL's TMP96C141)
- IZ register: LD_IZ_BC, SUB_IZ_imm16, LD_C_IZL
- Zero-extend: EXTZ_WA, EXTZ_BC
- Stack-relative: LD_A/XBC/XDE_pXSP_d, LD_pXSP_d_A/XDE, ADD_pXSP_d_XWA
- QIZH access: LD_QIZH_0, LD_A/C_QIZH, INC_1_QIZH, CP_QIZH_4
- Register moves: LD_XDE_XIZ, LD_BC_QBC/QDE, LD_XBC_pXDE_BC
- Memory auto-increment: LD_pXWA_plus_BC, ADD_XHL_pXWA_plus, DJNZ_DE

title: Mines: Only Mines Game button should activate game from DISK MENU
notes: At the DISK MENU, pressing other buttons also activates the Mines game. Need to ensure that ONLY the Mines Game button (specific event/button index) activates the game, while other buttons retain their original firmware behavior. The Mines_Handler in startup.s likely needs to check which button was pressed before activating.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Previous fix (XWA object_id check) was incorrect. User reports: (1) many DISK MENU buttons open incorrect screens, (2) the Mines Game option itself opens a floppy save/load test dialog instead of the game. This suggests the handler registration or delegation is fundamentally broken — the problem is not just filtering events, but that the entire handler 0x016A dispatch is disrupted by replacing the original 13-record table with our 1-record table.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: STATUS (Feb 21): Most recent Mines commit (88d6823) attempted to fix this by checking which DISK MENU entry was selected. However, per the earlier comment, the fundamental problem is deeper — replacing the original 13-record handler 0x016A table with a 1-record table disrupts all DISK MENU dispatch, not just Mines activation. This remains open and needs a different approach: possibly appending to the existing table rather than replacing it, or using a different handler registration strategy.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Root cause identified: Mines_Handler was delegating non-activation events to table_A[0x00DC] (the default DISK MENU lifecycle handler), which processes them as standard DISK MENU operations — showing 'FD SAVE/LOAD TEST' dialog. Fix: removed the entire delegate path. Handler 0x016A is extension-ROM exclusive (main firmware leaves it vacant), so ignoring non-activation events is safe. Also removed the XWA object_id check (0x016A0000) which may have been filtering out valid events — ClassProc already routes correctly by sub-index.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Revised fix: previous change removed the delegate entirely, which broke DISK MENU lifecycle — only 1 item visible, all buttons triggered activation (any 0x01C00008 event hit .Lmh_activate). Root cause: delegate is REQUIRED for lifecycle events (item enumeration, focus, redraw); only activation events (0x01C00008, 0x01E0009C) should skip delegation. Also removed XWA check. Added debug logging: XBC→0x200050, XWA→0x200058.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Built and committed 13-record handler table fix. Record[5]=Mines_Handler matches firmware's 0x016A0005 dispatch. Dummy_Return stubs for records 0-4,6-12. DISK MENU slot link updated to 0x016A0005. Ready for testing.

title: DS3613GF-3BA: false ALGO SELECT from alt param write format
description: The cmd 0x01 handler misidentified alt param write sub-packets (LABEL_0387E6) as algorithm selection. The data[2]==0x08 check was wrong — 0x08 is the sub-command prefix for alt param writes, not an algo select indicator. data[4] contained DSP register addresses (0x00-0x0F), not algorithm IDs. This caused: (1) false 'ALGO SELECT ch1 = 9 (SINGLE DELAY)' when editing REVERB TIME, (2) corrupted m_channel_algo causing wrong effect names on subsequent coefficient writes. Fixed by removing all ALGO SELECT detection from cmd 0x01 and properly decoding both standard and alt param write formats using the byte before 0x0A as discriminator: 0x00=standard, 0x21/0x25=alt.
close_reason: Fixed: removed false ALGO SELECT from cmd 0x01, properly decode standard and alt param write formats

title: Boot: Document peripheral initialization order
description: Trace initialization of all peripherals in boot sequence order: serial channels (SC0/SC1), timers (T0-TB), interrupts (INTA/INT0), FDC (0x110000), LCD controller (IC206), any other I/O. Document register values written during init.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
Sub CPU peripheral initialization order (from boot ROM at 0xFF8290):

**1. Watchdog (first, for safety):**
- WDMOD_REAL (0x110) = 0x00 (disable)
- WDCR_REAL (0x111) = 0xB1

**2. Interrupt Controller:**
- INT_CTRL (0x10A) = 0x04

**3. Port Function Controls (GPIO setup):**
- P0FC (0x07) = 0xFF, P1FC (0x0B) = 0xFF, P2FC (0x0F) = 0xFF
- P7 (0x1C) = 0xFF, P7FC (0x1F) = 0x07, P7CR (0x1E) = 0x78
- P8 (0x20) = 0x3B, P8FC (0x23) = 0x3F, P8CR (0x22) = 0xFF
- PA (0x28) = 0xFF, PAFC (0x2B) = 0x08
- PB (0x2C) = 0xFF, PBFC (0x2F) = 0x1F

**4. Interrupt/Status Registers:**
- INTTC01 (0x30) = 0x03
- Registers 0x32-0x3F configured for serial

**5. Extended Port Registers:**
- P8_FC_LO (0x44) = 0xFF
- P8_FC_EXT (0x47) = 0x18
- P8_FC_HI (0x46) = 0x07
- PE_DATA (0x68) = 0x00, PE_CR (0x6A) = 0xFF

**6. 8-bit Timers:**
- TREG0 (0x84) = 0x1D, TREG1 (0x85) = 0x1D
- T8RUN (0x82) = 0x00
- T23MOD (0x88) = 0x0A, T23FFCR (0x89) = 0x10
- TREG2 (0x8A) = 0x40, TREG3 (0x8B) = 0x20
- T01FFCR (0x81) = 0x00, T01MOD (0x80) bit 1 set

**7. 16-bit Timer 4:**
- T4MOD (0x98) = 0x05, T4FFCR (0x99) = 0x00
- CAP4H (0x9F) = 0x00, CAP4L (0x9E) = 0x00, then set bit 7

**8. Serial Channels:**
- SER0_MOD (0xD2) = 0x01, SER0_CR (0xD1) = 0x00
- SER0_BAUD (0xD3) masked with 0xCF then 0xF0
- SER1_MOD (0xD6) = 0x29 then modified, SER1_CR (0xD5) = 0x00
- SER1_BAUD (0xD7) masked similarly

**9. Port F (after serial):**
- PORT_FC_4 (0xF6) = 0x00

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
**Main CPU Peripheral Initialization Order (RESET_HANDLER at 0xEF03C6):**

**1. Watchdog Timer (first for safety):**
- WDMOD = 0x00 (disable)
- WDCR = 0xB1

**2. Clock Configuration:**
- CLKMOD = 0x04

**3. Port Configuration (GPIO setup):**
```
PF = 0x00, PFFC = 0x73 (control panel enabled, MIDI disabled), PFCR = 0x15
PB = masked with 0xF0
P8 bit 3 cleared, PF bit 2 cleared
P2FC = 0xFF, P3FC = 0xFF
P7 = 0xFF, P7FC = 0x1F, P7CR = 0x00
PA = 0xFE, PAFC = 0x08
PB = 0xFF, PBFC = 0x1F
PC = 0x03, PCFC = 0x00, PCCR = 0x02
PD = 0x00, PDFC = 0x06, PDCR = 0x11
PE = 0x00, PEFC = 0x42, PECR = 0x20
PH = 0x00, PHFC = 0x1E, PHCR = 0x09
PZ = 0xFF, PZCR = 0x03
```

**4. 8-bit Timers:**
- T01MOD = 0x1D, T23MOD = 0x1D
- T02FFCR = 0x00
- TREG0 = 0x0A, TREG1 = 0x10, TRDC = 0x00
- T8RUN bit 1 set

**5. 16-bit Timer 4:**
- T4MOD = 0x05, T4FFCR = 0x00, T16CR = 0x00
- TREG4L = 0x0001, TREG5L = 0x3D09
- T16RUN bits 7 and 0 set

**6. Memory Controller Setup:**
(See kn5000-cav for detailed MSAR/MAMR/BxCS values)

**7. DRAM Initialization:**
- Delay loops + DRAM1REF/CRL/CRH configuration

**8. Serial Channel 0:**
- SC0MOD = 0x29, SC0CR = 0x00
- BR0CR masked

**9. DAC (Audio Output):**
- DAREG0 = 0xFF, DAREG1 = 0xFF, DADRV = 0x03

**10. Stack Pointer:**
- XSP = 0x00000C00

**11. Post-Init (calls to subroutines):**
- Seems_to_copy_some_data_buffers
- MainCPU_self_test_routines
- Get_Firmware_Version
- Some_VGA_setup (if running boot ROM)
- Interrupt priority setup (INTET01, INTET45)
- Region detection
- HD-AE5000 init (if present)
- Control panel button read

Both CPUs follow similar initialization pattern: watchdog → clock → ports → timers → serial → memory

Status: Well documented for both CPUs.

title: Boot: Document sub CPU startup handshake
description: Trace main CPU code that initializes sub CPU communication. Document: when sub CPU is released from reset, MicroDMA payload transfer trigger, latch communication at 0x120000 during boot, how main CPU knows sub CPU is ready. Cross-reference with SubCPU tasks.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
Sub CPU boot sequence documented from boot ROM disassembly:

**Reset Vector (0xFFFEE0):**
- Loads PC from 0xFFFF00-0xFFFF02: jumps to BOOT_INIT at 0xFF8290

**BOOT_INIT sequence (0xFF8290):**
1. Disable watchdog: WDMOD_REAL=0, WDCR_REAL=0xB1
2. Set interrupt control: INT_CTRL=0x04
3. Configure all port function controls (P0-PB)
4. Configure interrupt/serial registers (0x30-0x3F area)
5. Configure extended port registers (P8_FC, PE)
6. Set up timers (TREG0-3, T01MOD, T23MOD, T4MOD)
7. Configure memory controller blocks (MSAR0-5, MAMR0-5, B0-B5 CSL/CSH)
8. Initialize serial channels (SER0, SER1)
9. Configure DRAM controller (refresh, timing)
10. Set stack pointer: XSP = 0x05A2
11. Copy interrupt trampolines: COPY_VECTORS (0xFF8F6C -> 0x0400, 225 bytes)
12. Enable interrupts: EI 0
13. Call INIT_MEMORY_TEST (0xFF8956)
14. Call INIT_DMA_SERIAL (0xFF85AE)
15. Call INIT_TONE_GEN (0xFF84A8)
16. Enter MAIN_LOOP

**MAIN_LOOP handshake:**
- Clears bit 6 of SUBCPU_STATUS_FLAGS (0x04FE)
- Polls bit 6 waiting for payload ready signal
- When ready: EI 6, calls PAYLOAD_ENTRY (0x0400)
- Continuously updates interrupt control based on serial status

**How main CPU knows sub CPU is ready:**
- Sub CPU sets bit 0 of INTERCPU_STATUS (0x34) when ready
- Main CPU polls bit 4 which sub CPU monitors
- E3 command sets bit 6 of SUBCPU_STATUS_FLAGS to signal payload ready

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
**Main CPU side of handshake (inferred from memory map and architecture):**

The inter-CPU communication latch at 0x120000 is used bidirectionally:
- Main CPU writes commands to 0x120000
- Sub CPU reads from same address via DMA
- Handshaking via INTERCPU_STATUS register bits

**Command Protocol (from Sub CPU boot ROM):**
```
0x00-0x1F: General commands (bits 7-5 = handler, bits 4-0 = length-1)
0xE1: DMA transfer type 1 (multi-stage, 6 bytes)
0xE2: DMA transfer type 2 (payload, 10 bytes)
0xE3: Signal payload ready (triggers bit 6 of SUBCPU_STATUS_FLAGS)
```

**Payload Transfer Sequence:**
1. Main CPU loads payload into RAM
2. Main CPU sends E2 command with address/size parameters
3. Sub CPU receives via DMA, stores to destination
4. Main CPU sends E3 to signal all payload data ready
5. Sub CPU sets bit 6 of status flags
6. Sub CPU boot ROM detects bit 6, calls PAYLOAD_ENTRY (0x0400)

**Note:** The main CPU firmware at 0xE00000+ likely has a routine that performs this payload transfer during boot. The exact location needs tracing from the main CPU reset handler's call sequence.

Status: Well documented from Sub CPU perspective. Main CPU side needs firmware tracing to identify exact routine locations.

title: LLVM backend: Replace all raw encoding wrappers with semantic instructions
description: 1,345 instructions across all 6 ROMs use raw encoding wrappers (sriXN, erpXN, sd8XN, etc.) instead of semantic TLCS-900 mnemonics. These are opaque byte sequences that need proper instruction definitions in the LLVM backend and converter tiers to emit them. Breakdown: CP (696), AND (116), BIT (91), ADD (86), OR (58), PUSHW (55), LD/LDW (54), STCF/LDCF/XORCF (36), and others. Addressing modes involved: Q-bank registers (erp), register+register indexed (sri), stack-relative (sri with XSP), SFR direct (sd8), 16/24-bit direct (sd16/sd24), post-increment (spi).
close_reason: Closed

This is prerequisite for accurate audio emulation in MAME.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
Research complete. Key findings from SubCPU firmware analysis:
- Tone Gen uses register-indirect protocol: write address to 0x100000, data to 0x100002
- P6.7 (port 0x18 bit 7) controls A23 for tone gen access
- 64 voices with 28 parameters each, organized in 4 phases (A/B/C + channel init)
- Voice event input at 0x110000/0x110002 (status bit 0 = data ready)
- Command values: 0xA200 (base params), 0xA280 (secondary), 0xFF00 (note-on), 0x7E00 (reset)
- ToneGen_SetupPolyVoice copies 34-byte template, applies velocity/effect routing
- ToneGen_Calc_Pitch: MIDI note + 36 offset, ROM lookup tables for frequency/velocity
- Strict timing: NOPs between address and data writes (A23 latch timing)
Next step: Create documentation page for tone generator register interface.

title: HDAE5000: Analyze PPI interface at 0x160000
description: Reverse engineer the 8255 PPI interface used to communicate with HD-AE5000. Document port A/B/C assignments, control register settings, and data transfer protocol. Cross-reference with main CPU firmware at addresses accessing 0x160000.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
HDAE5000 PPI Interface Analysis (from main CPU firmware):

**PPI Port Addresses:**
```
HDAE5000_PPI__PORT_A      = 0x160000  ; Output port
HDAE5000_PPI__PORT_B      = 0x160002  ; Input port  
HDAE5000_PPI__PORT_C      = 0x160004  ; Output port (control signals)
HDAE5000_PPI__CONTROL_REG = 0x160006  ; PPI control register
HDAE5000_ROM__BASE_ADDR   = 0x280000  ; HDAE5000 ROM (512KB)
```

**PPI Initialization (HDAE5000_Parport_Setup at 0xEF4BCC):**
1. Bus control: B5CSL = 0x66 (bus timing for HDAE5000)
2. PPI control: 0x82 - decoded as:
   - Mode set (bit 7 = 1)
   - Port A: Mode 0, Output
   - Port B: Mode 0, Input  
   - Port C: Output (both upper and lower nibbles)
3. Port A = 0x00 (clear output port)
4. Port C = 0x00, then 0x0F (pulse control signals)
5. Delay loop (0xDBBA0 iterations)
6. Port C = 0x00 (clear control signals)
7. Poll Port B bit 0 until clear (wait for HDAE5000 ready)

**Port C Control Signals (from code at 0xEF4A6A-0xEF4B53):**
- Bit 0: Strobe signal (SET 0, then RES 0)
- Bit 1: Secondary strobe
- Bit 2: Data direction/select (CHG 2 toggles)
- Bit 3: Another control signal (CHG 3 toggles)
- Values 0x01, 0x02, 0x04, 0x08 written individually

**Data Transfer Protocol:**
- Port A is used for data output (byte values written)
- Port B is used for status input (bit 0 = busy/ready)
- Port C provides handshaking control signals

**HD-AE5000 Detection (0xEF05B0):**
```asm
BIT 0, (PE)           ; Check PE port bit 0
JR NZ, skip_hdae      ; If set, HD-AE5000 NOT present
CALR Get_Area_Region_Code
CP L, 4               ; Check region code
CALL NZ, HDAE5000_Parport_Setup ; Init if present
```

The HD-AE5000 presence is detected via PE port bit 0 (active low).

Next steps: Update reverse-engineering.md HDAE5000 section with actual PPI port addresses (0x160000-0x160006), control register value (0x82), Port C signal assignments, detection code (PE bit 0), and initialization sequence. Currently shows generic 'Tasks:' list.

title: Document ROM interleaving formats for all ROM chips
notes:
Different ROM components use different interleaving formats. This caused confusion during table_data bitmap extraction.

**Formats discovered:**

| ROM | Interleaving | Notes |
|-----|--------------|-------|
| Main CPU | None (single chip) | 2MB linear |
| Sub CPU Boot | None (single chip) | 128KB linear |
| Sub CPU Payload | None (sent by maincpu) | 192KB linear |
| Table Data | 16-bit WORD-level | odd.ic1 + even.ic3, alternating 16-bit words |
| HDAE5000 | None (single chip) | 512KB linear |

The table_data ROM is NOT byte-interleaved but WORD-interleaved:
- Correct: even[0:2] + odd[0:2] + even[2:4] + odd[2:4] ...
- Wrong: even[0] + odd[0] + even[1] + odd[1] ...

This should be documented in:
1. CLAUDE.md for developer reference
2. rom-reconstruction.md (partially done)
3. Hardware architecture docs

Reference: kn5000_table_data.rom combination analysis
close_reason: Documented ROM interleaving formats in hardware-architecture.md (new section with table and explanation) and clarified storage-subsystem.md. All 6 ROM types documented.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: STATUS (Feb 21): ROM interleaving formats are well-documented in issue notes. Table data 16-bit WORD-level interleaving was the key discovery. Now that table_data is at 100% byte-match, the interleaving handling is confirmed correct. This issue is primarily about adding the documentation to CLAUDE.md and hardware architecture docs — the knowledge exists but isn't formally documented in all the right places.

title: Document jump tables in maincpu ROM
notes:
The maincpu ROM contains numerous jump tables used for dispatch. Found patterns include:

**Indirect call patterns:**
- CALL T, XHL - calls through XHL register
- CALL T, XIX - calls through XIX register
- JP T, XIX + WA - indexed jump with WA offset
- JP T, XIX + BC - indexed jump with BC offset
- JP T, XIX + DE - indexed jump with DE offset

**Known jump tables:**
1. HANDLE_UPDATE_OFFSETS (0xE00178) - 16-bit offset table for update file handling
2. LABEL_EF0D64 - 3-entry address table for state machine
3. LABEL_EF0DA5 - 16-entry address table for sub-state handling
4. Large address table at line 36362 (~170 entries for handler dispatch)
5. Address tables at E1611A, E16128, E16136 (encoder handling)
6. Jump table at F97D8D with 12+ undisassembled target routines

**Work needed:**
- Label all jump tables with meaningful names
- Ensure all target routines are disassembled
- Document the purpose of each table
- Create cross-references in comments
close_reason: Phase 1 (table renaming) and Phase 2 (target classification + handler renaming) complete. All tables documented, all code targets have semantic labels. ~460 total labels renamed across 12 sed script batches. Remaining deep-dive work (semantic analysis, name corrections) can be tracked as separate issues.

title: Video: Document LCD controller IC206 (MN89304)
description: Research and document the MN89304 LCD controller. Find datasheet if available. Document: register map, supported video modes, pixel formats, timing parameters, interface to main CPU. Identify memory-mapped I/O addresses used to control the display.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
**MN89304 LCD Controller Analysis (from firmware reverse engineering):**

The MN89304 is a Panasonic LCD controller that provides **VGA-compatible register interface**.

**I/O Port Addresses (memory-mapped):**
```
0x3C0: Attribute Controller Address/Data
0x3C2: Misc Output Register (write)
0x3C3: VGA Enable  
0x3C4/3C5: Sequencer Address/Data
0x3C6: DAC Mask
0x3C8: DAC Write Address
0x3C9: DAC Data (palette RGB)
0x3CE/3CF: Graphics Controller Address/Data
0x3D4/3D5: CRTC Address/Data
0x3DA: Input Status 1
```

**Video Memory:** 0x1A0000

**Display Characteristics (from CRTC register values):**
- Horizontal Total: 0x65 (101 chars)
- Horizontal Display End: 0x27 (39 chars = 320 pixels at 8 pixels/char)
- Vertical Display End: 0xEF (239 lines → 240 lines)
- Resolution: **320x240 pixels**
- Dot clock: 25 MHz (from Misc Output = 0xE3)
- Refresh: ~60 Hz

**Color Depth:**
- EGA-compatible 16-color palette at indices 0-15
- Full 256-color DAC palette programmable via 0x3C8/0x3C9
- 8-bit color depth (256 colors)

**Key Initialization (Some_VGA_setup at 0xEF55A7):**
1. Global enable (0x3C3 = 0x01)
2. Misc output for timing (0x3C2 = 0xE3)
3. Sequencer reset and clock config
4. Graphics controller mode setup
5. CRTC timing parameters
6. Attribute controller palette
7. DAC palette colors

**Reference:** See kn5000-t75 for complete initialization sequence.

Status: Documented from firmware. Datasheet not found but VGA compatibility confirmed.

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text: REOPENING: Website documentation not updated. Next steps: Update reverse-engineering.md 'LCD Controller (MN89304)' section with documented register addresses, initialization sequence, and video modes. Currently just 'Tasks:' placeholders.

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text: Website updated: reverse-engineering.md 'LCD Controller (MN89304)' section now documents all I/O port addresses, display configuration table, initialization sequence steps, and CRTC timing registers. Confirmed VGA compatibility.

title: Update: Identify Flash ROM chip types
description: Document the Flash ROM chips used for updatable storage. From service manual: identify manufacturer, part numbers, capacity, sector size, command set (JEDEC, AMD, Intel). Chips at 0xE00000 (program) and 0x300000 (custom data).
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
**Flash ROM Chip Identification (from service manual schematic):**

**Program ROM (0xE00000 - 2MB total):**
| IC | Part Number | Type | Size | Bus |
|----|-------------|------|------|-----|
| IC4 | QV1GFKN5KAX1 | Flash ROM | 8Mbit (1MB) | EVEN bytes |
| IC6 | QV1GFKN5KAX1 | Flash ROM | 8Mbit (1MB) | ODD bytes |

**Custom Data Flash (0x300000 - 1MB):**
| IC | Part Number | Type | Size |
|----|-------------|------|------|
| IC19 | QV1GFKN5KAX1 | Flash ROM | 8Mbit (1MB) |

**Chip Analysis:**
- Part number "QV1GFKN5KAX1" appears to be Matsushita/Panasonic custom
- QV prefix = Panasonic semiconductor
- KN5K suffix likely indicates KN5000-specific mask/configuration
- 8Mbit = 1 megabyte capacity per chip
- Program ROM uses 2 chips in even/odd configuration for 16-bit bus

**Memory Organization:**
- IC4 (EVEN): Addresses 0xE00000, 0xE00002, 0xE00004... (even bytes)
- IC6 (ODD): Addresses 0xE00001, 0xE00003, 0xE00005... (odd bytes)
- Combined: 2MB addressable as 16-bit words

**Command Set:**
Likely AMD/JEDEC compatible (common for era). Flash programming routines in firmware would reveal exact command sequences.

**Related Firmware:**
- FLASH_MEM_UPDATE routine at 0xEF???? handles programming
- Issues kn5000-1tn and kn5000-dkx track Flash program/erase algorithms

Status: Chip identification documented. Command set needs firmware analysis.

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text: REOPENING: Website documentation not updated. Next steps: Update reverse-engineering.md 'Flash ROM Chips' section with documented chip types (QV1GFKN5KAX1), locations (IC4/IC6), capacities, and sector sizes. Also add to hardware-architecture.md if not present.

title: Disassemble and document FA9945 (EventDispatch_Direct)
description:
FA9945 is in .byte form (line 304367+ in kn5000_v10_program.s). Called by F98697 (KeyPress_StateDispatch) with XWA=target workspace, XBC=event code (typically 0x1C00038), XDE=event param.

Entry point: 0xFA9945 (maincpu ROM)
Purpose: Direct event dispatch — routes an event to a registered workspace handler. This is the final step in the key press → event chain.

Context:
- F98697 always calls FA9945 with XBC=0x1C00038 and XWA=0xFFFFFFFF (broadcast)
- GroupBoxProc (feature demo dialog) handles 0x1C00038 → GroupBoxProc_StartSSFPresentation
- FA9945 is similar to FA9660/FA9D58 but with specific dispatch semantics

Raw bytes (FA9945):
bf ea 37 d7 fa 04 bf 0c 62 bf 10 61 bf 14 60 d8
ac 1d a7 1e ef d2 36 ec 02 21 d2 34 ec 02 22 d9
f2 6e 12 d8 ac 1d d4 1d ef af 14 20 af 10 21 af
0c 22 78 f3 00 af 0c 20 bf 02 60 e8 cc ff 00 00
00 bf 06 41 af 02 20 e8 ...

Related functions:
- FA9660: SendEvent(target, event, param) — send to specific workspace
- FA9D58: BroadcastEvent(event, param) — send to all listeners
- FA9945: DirectDispatch — called via jp from F98697, likely equivalent to FA9660 with 0xFFFFFFFF target

Task: Use unidasm to decode FA9945, identify calling convention and dispatch mechanism, convert .byte to native LLVM assembly, add symbolic name and documentary comments. Cross-reference with FA9660 and FA9D58 to understand the event dispatch family.
close_reason: EventDispatch_Direct (FA9945) decoded: 306 bytes converted to native/byte mix with 7 internal labels, doc comments. 100% byte match all 6 ROMs. Commit 52850e4.

title: LLVM TLCS-900: Variable shift SLA Rx,Ry generates wrong encoding
description: When the C compiler optimizes (type==4||type==6||type==0xE) into a bitmask check with variable shift, the generated SLA Rx,Ry instruction encodes incorrectly as SLA 2,Ry (fixed shift by 2 instead of variable shift). The LLVM backend should emit LD A,count_reg then SLA A,dest_reg for variable shifts. Currently worked around with volatile in apploader/src/fat16.c.
close_reason: Fixed in LLVM commit 6a811cd: variable shifts now expand to COPY+SLA_A_32 via EmitInstrWithCustomInserter. Volatile workaround removed from apploader.

title: LLVM bug: XSP register encoded as 0x1D instead of 0xFD in F3-prefix d16 mode
description: When lda xsp, (xsp + d16) requires the F3 addressing prefix (displacement > 127), the LLVM backend encodes XSP as register index 7 (byte 0x1D = 7<<2|1) instead of the correct register file address 0x3F (byte 0xFD = 0x3F<<2|1). The d8 form (BF prefix) works correctly. Affects all lda xsp, (xsp +/- N) where N > 127. Workaround: use .byte for these instructions.
close_reason: Fixed in LLVM commit 0f17355: emitMemPrefix() now uses 0xE0+(BaseReg<<2)+1 for F3 prefix d16 mode byte. 3 .byte workarounds removed from fd_test_code.s, all 6 ROMs 100% byte match.

title: LLVM: Fix bug #10 — register x/y swap on inlining
notes: LLVM TLCS-900 backend bug #10: When functions are inlined, IX and IY registers get swapped in the generated code. Current workaround: use __attribute__((noinline)) on affected functions (e.g., tile_vram_ptr in Mines). This is one of 2 remaining active bugs in the TLCS-900 backend. Tracked in Mines memory (llvm-encoding-bugs.md).
close_reason: No longer reproducible. Original analysis used wrong register-parameter mapping. Code generation is correct with the XDE-first calling convention.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: STATUS (Feb 21): Bug #10 remains UNFIXED in the LLVM TLCS-900 backend. Workaround still active in Mines video.c (line 82): __attribute__((noinline)) on tile_vram_ptr(). Recent LLVM work (10 commits) focused on LDIR/LDDR block transfers, peephole optimizations, frame pointer support, and 8-bit register encoding — none address this bug.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Starting investigation of Bug #10 after fixing Bug #11.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Investigation complete: Bug #10 appears to be NO LONGER REPRODUCIBLE. The original analysis incorrectly assumed XBC=first parameter, but the calling convention (TLCS900CallingConv.td) has always been XDE-first, XBC-second: CCAssignToReg<[XDE, XBC, XIX, XIY]>. With this corrected mapping, the generated code for inlined tile_vram_ptr is correct: mul xbc,2560 multiplies dst_y (in XBC) by row stride, and sla xde,3 shifts dst_x (in XDE) for column offset. Constant-folded test set_tile(5,10,42) produces correct address 0x1A6428 = VRAM_BASE + 10*2560 + 5*8. Recommend: remove __attribute__((noinline)) workaround from Mines video.c and verify on MAME.

title: LLVM parallel build: Phase 3 - Progressive native instruction replacement
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Documenting Phase 3 completion.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
COMPLETED: Phase 3 fully implemented in scripts/asl_to_llvm.py. All tiers done:

Step 3a: Refactored convert_all() back to per-instruction .byte emission (from 16-byte chunks). Labels emitted as .set directives. 100% byte-match verified.

Step 3b Tier Results (all 100% byte-match verified):
- T1: Zero-operand (NOP, RET, RETI, HALT) — 5,284 native instructions
- T2: 32-bit reg-imm loads (LD XWA,#imm) — 8,556 native instructions  
- T3: Reg-reg ALU + single-reg ops + INC/DEC — 32,792 native instructions
- T4: Reg-imm ALU (ADD/SUB/CP/AND/OR/XOR/ADC/SBC) — 11,751 native instructions
- T5: Unconditional JP/CALL with hardcoded addresses — 14,952 native instructions
- T6: PUSH/POP 32-bit registers — 9,207 native instructions
- T7: Register-indirect LD (load/store via XBC/XHL/XSP etc) — 8,067 native instructions

Final stats: ~91K native instruction lines (24.9%), 592,545 native bytes (28.3% of ROM), 1,504,607 fallback bytes (71.7%).

Key findings:
- LLVM pads JR/JRL/CALR to 4 bytes (NOP padding) — cannot convert relative branches
- 16-bit reg loads use different encoding (3-byte ROM vs 4-byte LLVM)
- Short-form immediates (values 0-7) use 2-byte encoding LLVM doesn't produce
- ROM byte verification essential: 771 ASL disassembly mismatches caught in T3 alone
- Labels in block overflow regions at wrong addresses — use numeric JP/CALL targets

title: MAME: Implement MIDI output (TX0)
description: Currently only MIDI RX0 is connected in the MAME driver. TX0 (MIDI output) is marked TODO at line 834 of kn5000.cpp. The KN5000 can output MIDI from its sequencer, accompaniment engine, and keyboard. Implementing MIDI output would make the emulator useful as a virtual MIDI instrument. Need to: (1) wire TX0 serial output to a midi_port device, (2) configure baud rate (31250 standard MIDI), (3) test with a MIDI monitor to verify note output from keybed.
close_reason: UART mode implemented (TX+RX) in tmp94c241_serial.cpp. MIDI output wired (TX0→mdout). Committed de4025339e8 + 21c29d3f2f4. Needs runtime testing with MIDI monitor.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: RESEARCH COMPLETE: Detailed analysis of TX0 MIDI output implementation requirements. See agent output for full plan. Key finding: UART mode (8-bit, baud rate generator) is NOT YET IMPLEMENTED in tmp94c241_serial.cpp — the serial device only handles synchronous I/O interface mode (mode 0). UART mode support is the primary blocker.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: RESEARCH COMPLETE (Mar 14): Primary blocker is NOT the driver wiring (trivial one-liner) but that tmp94c241_serial_device lacks UART mode implementation. Currently only synchronous I/O mode 0 works. UART mode (mode 2, SC0MOD=0x29) needed for MIDI 31250 baud. Firmware configures BR0CR=0x08 for correct baud rate. Implementation requires: (1) UART TX bit-shifting in timer_callback (10 bits: start+8data+stop), (2) UART RX with 16x oversampling, (3) one-line mdout wiring in kn5000.cpp. This also means MIDI INPUT (already wired) likely doesn't work either. Reference drivers: akai/mpc3000.cpp.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: PROGRESS: UART mode implemented in tmp94c241_serial.cpp (8-bit TX/RX with 16x oversampling). MIDI output wired in kn5000.cpp (TX0→mdout midi_port). Committed: de4025339e8 (UART), 21c29d3f2f4 (MIDI wiring). Build verified. Testing pending.

title: LLVM converter: Native remaining misc instructions (~2,000+ instructions)
description:
## Goal
Convert remaining miscellaneous instruction types from .byte fallback to native LLVM.

## Instruction forms and counts

### SCC cc, reg — 218 instances
Set register to 1 if condition true, 0 if false.
- 3-byte: prefix + 0x70+cc  (prefix encodes register)
- Example: scc nz, a

### DJNZ reg, target — 129 instances
Decrement and jump if not zero (loop instruction).
- 3-byte: prefix + 0x1C + d8
- Already supported as PrefixDJNZ format in MCCodeEmitter
- Needs PC-relative target label resolution (like JR)

### EI level — 301 instances
Enable interrupts at specified level.
- 2-byte: 0x06, level (0-7)
- Already supported as SingleByteImm8 format

### RETD imm16 — 68 instances
Return and deallocate stack frame.
- 3-byte: 0x0F, d16_LE
- Already supported as SingleByteImm8 format (special case for 0x0F)

### RET / RETI — ~621 instances (some already native?)
- RET: 1-byte (0x0E) — should be in Tier 1 NATIVE_ZERO_OPS
- RETI: 1-byte (0x07) — should be in Tier 1
- Check if these are actually already handled; if 549 remain as fallback, some forms may differ

### EX F,F' — 31 instances
Exchange flag registers. 1-byte: 0x16.

### MUL/MULS/DIV/DIVS — ~1,989 instances total
- MULS WA,reg: 2-byte prefix + 0x08 (multiply signed)
- MUL WA,reg: 2-byte prefix + 0x00 (multiply unsigned)  
- DIVS WA,reg: 2-byte prefix + 0x09
- DIV WA,reg: 2-byte prefix + 0x01
- These may need new converter tiers since they're specialized reg-reg ops

### LDIR/LDIRW — ~583 instances
Block transfer instructions (repeat).
- LDIR: 2-byte (0x80, 0x10)
- LDIRW: variant
- Already supported as BlockTransfer format

## LLVM backend status
Most of these formats are already supported in the MCCodeEmitter:
- PrefixDJNZ, SingleByteImm8, PrefixShift, BlockTransfer, SingleByte
- Some may need new converter logic but no backend changes

## Priority
Lower priority than LD/LDA/CP which have 10-50x more instances. Implement after the big categories.

## Verification
1. Regenerate, build, compare_roms → 100.00%

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: DESCRIPTION: ASL macros (RegObjTabl, RegTitle, _VGA_SEQUENCER, VGA_COLOR_CRTC, etc.) expand to 6-40 bytes of packed instructions emitted as a single .byte line. 1,035 commented lines (28,925 bytes, ~5,000 instructions). Fix: parse the packed bytes using instruction length info from the ASL listing, split into individual instructions, and convert each one. Related to kn5000-28dn — same root cause (multi-instruction byte sequences treated as atomic).
