title: Testing: Establish emulation validation procedures
notes:
Define testing procedures for validating MAME emulation accuracy.

## Testing Categories

### Boot Sequence Validation
- ROM checksum verification
- Peripheral init order matches real hardware
- Sub CPU payload transfer timing

### Display Validation
- Boot splash appearance
- UI element positioning
- Font rendering accuracy
- Color reproduction

### Audio Validation
- Basic tone generation
- MIDI input response
- Effects processing
- Timing/latency

### Input Validation
- Button press response
- Rotary encoder behavior
- Analog controller range

## Test Data Needed
- Screenshots from real hardware
- Audio recordings
- Timing measurements
- Logic analyzer captures

## Success Criteria
- Documented test procedures
- Baseline captures from real hardware
- Automated comparison where possible
close_reason: Automated test suite created and verified: boot sequence (5 screenshots, crash detection), menu navigation (4 menus via Lua), display mode (320x240 LCD). All 5 checks pass.

title: LLVM converter: 2 remaining TMP94C241-specific LD (XIX+d8), imm8 fallbacks
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: ROM addresses 0xF20DAD and 0xF20DCD. Encoding: 0xF1,0x5C,0x8F,{0xB8|0xB0}. The TMP94C241 supports an alternate encoding for LD (XIX+d8), imm8 using the 0xF1 extended prefix. LLVM's assembler produces the standard 0xBC prefix encoding (functionally identical, different bytes). Fixing requires adding a TMP94C241 alternate encoding mode to the LLVM backend — not justified for 2 instructions. Leave as .byte.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Starting work. Will investigate the exact encoding to determine best approach.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: RESOLVED: The ASL comments were wrong — bytes 0xF1,0x5C,0x8F,0xB8/0xB0 are SET 0,(0x8F5C) and RES 0,(0x8F5C), not LD (XIX+5Ch),imm8. Extended convert_db() comment parser to handle ADDR: MNEMONIC format and opcode-based guessing. Both instructions now emit native setda/resda. 100% ROM byte match verified.

title: LLVM converter: Native 8/16-bit register immediate loads (~11,834 instructions)
description:
## Goal
Convert LD r8,#imm8 and LD r16,#imm16 from .byte fallback to native LLVM instructions.

## Instruction forms

### LD r8, #imm8 (2-byte: 0x20+r, imm8) — 5,533 instances
- Opcode range: 0x20-0x27 (W=0, A=1, B=2, C=3, D=4, E=5, H=6, L=7)
- Example: .byte 0x21, 0x00 → ld a, 0x0

### LD r16, #imm16 (3-byte: 0x30+r, imm16_LE) — 6,301 instances  
- Opcode range: 0x30-0x37 (WA=0, BC=1, DE=2, HL=3, IX=4, IY=5, IZ=6, SP=7)
- Example: .byte 0x33, 0x00, 0x00 → ld hl, 0x0

## LLVM backend status
- LD r8,#imm8: Already supported as SingleByteImm8 format (opcode range in _SIMPLE_LENGTHS confirmed as 2 bytes)
- LD r16,#imm16: Already supported as SingleByteRegImm format with 16-bit immediate
- Both formats are already encodable by the MCCodeEmitter

## Converter changes needed
File: scripts/asl_to_llvm.py, in try_convert_native()

### Tier 11a: LD r8, #imm8
- Check: nbytes == 2, 0x20 <= rom_bytes[0] <= 0x27, mnem_upper in ('LD',)
- Register index: rom_bytes[0] - 0x20
- Register names: {0:'w', 1:'a', 2:'b', 3:'c', 4:'d', 5:'e', 6:'h', 7:'l'}
- Immediate: rom_bytes[1]
- Emit: ld <r8>, 0x<imm>

### Tier 11b: LD r16, #imm16
- Check: nbytes == 3, 0x30 <= rom_bytes[0] <= 0x37, mnem_upper in ('LD', 'LDW')
- Register index: rom_bytes[0] - 0x30
- Register names: {0:'wa', 1:'bc', 2:'de', 3:'hl', 4:'ix', 5:'iy', 6:'iz', 7:'sp'}
- Immediate: rom_bytes[1] | (rom_bytes[2] << 8)
- Emit: ld <r16>, 0x<imm>

## Verification
1. Regenerate: python scripts/asl_to_llvm.py maincpu/kn5000_v10_program.asm
2. Build: make rebuilt_ROMs/kn5000_v10_program.llvm.rom
3. Match: python scripts/compare_roms.py → 100.00%
4. Spot-check: grep -cP '^\s+ld [a-z]+, 0x' in output

## Caution
- The 2-byte LD check overlaps with Tier 3 (reg-reg). Place AFTER Tier 3 or verify rom_bytes[0] range.
- Don't confuse with Tier 2 (5-byte LD r32,#imm32) — different byte count.
- PUSH r16 (0x28-0x2F) and POP r16 (0x48-0x4F) are adjacent opcodes — handle separately.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: COMPLETED: Implemented 8,200 native LD r8/r16 immediate instructions across 3 encoding forms: ldb (1,891 short-form 8-bit), ldw (2,372 short-form 16-bit), lds (3,937 small-immediate 0-7). Added 3 new LLVM instructions (LD8ri_short, LD16ri_short, LD16ri_small) with PrefixSmallImm format. Also added prefix-form conversion (Tiers 13-14) which matched 0 instructions since firmware always uses shorter encodings. Fallback: 131K→96K. ROM 100% byte match.

title: LLVM TLCS-900: LDA with displacement > d8 generates invalid encoding
description: When LTO + O2 constant-folds a base address (e.g. SECTOR_BUFFER at 0x200400) into an LDA instruction with large displacement, the backend generates invalid F3 ED prefix bytes that don't correspond to any real TLCS-900 instruction. The CPU can only do d8 displacement for register-indirect addressing. The backend should generate LD Rd,base_reg + ADD Rd,imm instead. Currently worked around with noinline/optnone on helper functions in apploader/src/fat16.c.
close_reason: Fixed in LLVM commit 59277bf: FixLargeDisp now handles MemStore/MemLoadDst with d16 overflow, eliminateFrameIndex checks d16 range, MCCodeEmitter validates d16 bounds. The noinline/optnone workaround in apploader can now be removed since FixLargeDisp handles the VRAM base address folding.

title: LLVM converter: drifted Fmm medley labels (4 fallbacks)
description:
Four .byte fallbacks for JRL/CALR to Fmm medley function labels:
- JRL T, FmmSmfMedleyFunc (lines 309196, 314038 as CALR)
- JRL T, FmmPdMedleyFunc (line 309212)
- CALR FmmDiskMedleySelectFunc (line 309220)

These labels exist in the LLVM output but at wrong addresses (drifted). The converter's address tracker accumulates errors before reaching these labels, so the computed branch offset doesn't match the ROM bytes.

Fix approach: trace the address tracking from the segment start to identify where the byte count goes wrong. Likely a mislabeled LABEL_XXXXXX (like the FE730F→FE7310 fix) or incorrect instruction size in get_instruction_size_from_rom().
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Progress: FmmDiskMedleySelectFunc was at the correct address — removed from DRIFTED_LABEL_NAMES (committed 88836dc). FmmSmfMedleyFunc and FmmPdMedleyFunc remain drifted by 425 and 2482 bytes respectively. The drift is caused by password_slot_routines.asm binclude ORG creating a segment boundary at F92C0E that cuts through medley.asm code. Converted forward-reference ORG+LABEL to EQU (committed 27e580a) which helps but doesn't fully fix the block buffer limit. Attempted ORIGINAL_ROM fallthrough for block overflow — abandoned due to double byte emission with padding segments. 3 fallbacks remain: JRL T FmmSmfMedleyFunc, JRL T FmmPdMedleyFunc, CALR FmmSmfMedleyFunc. RELEASED: blocked on fundamental block overflow design.

title: SubCPU: Trace payload transfer initialization
description: Find and document the main CPU code that initializes the sub CPU payload transfer. Identify: source address (payload location in main ROM), destination address (sub CPU memory), transfer size (192KB), DMA mode settings, and any handshaking with sub CPU before/during/after transfer.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
Findings from Sub CPU boot ROM analysis:

**Payload reception (Sub CPU side):**
- Boot ROM waits in MAIN_LOOP for bit 6 of SUBCPU_STATUS_FLAGS (0x04FE)
- E3 command (0xE3) sets this bit, signaling payload is ready
- When ready, EI 6 enables interrupt level 6
- Then calls PAYLOAD_ENTRY at 0x0400

**Pre-payload handshake:**
- INTERCPU_STATUS (0x34) bit 0 = sub CPU ready
- INTERCPU_STATUS (0x34) bit 4 = main CPU ready (polled by sub CPU)
- DMA_SYNC_FLAG (0x0516) tracks transfer state

**DMA transfer commands:**
- E1: Multi-stage DMA (6 bytes to CMD_E1_BUFFER at 0x0544)
- E2: Payload DMA (10 bytes to CMD_E2_BUFFER at 0x054A)
- Variable-length: Up to 32 bytes to CMD_DATA_BUFFER at 0x051E

**Transfer state machine (CMD_PROCESSING_STATE at 0x0518):**
- State 1: Process received data, dispatch to handler
- State 2: Secondary DMA using CMD_E1_BUFFER parameters
- State 3: Set completion flags (bit 7 of 0x0554)
- State 4: Clear ready flag, return to idle

**To complete this task:** Need to find main CPU code that sends these commands and trace payload source address in main ROM.

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
**Summary of Payload Transfer Protocol:**

**Sub CPU Boot ROM receives payload via:**
1. Main CPU sends E2 command with 10-byte parameter block containing:
   - Source address (main CPU memory)
   - Destination address (sub CPU memory) 
   - Transfer count
2. Sub CPU DMA controller transfers data in chunks
3. Main CPU sends E3 command when all data sent
4. Sub CPU detects E3, sets status flag, jumps to PAYLOAD_ENTRY (0x0400)

**Payload Size:** 192KB (kn5000_subprogram_v142.rom)

**Destination:** Sub CPU RAM, executed from 0x0400 onwards

**Main CPU side:** Not yet traced in firmware. The routine likely:
- Loads payload from ROM (probably in 0xE00000+ area)
- Sends E2 commands iteratively for 192KB
- Sends E3 to signal completion

**Note:** Sub CPU side fully documented. Main CPU sender routine location unknown but protocol behavior understood.

Status: Protocol documented. Main CPU routine location remains open question but doesn't block understanding.

title: LLVM: Replace ld_sril3/srib3/sriw3 with semantic register+displacement mnemonics
description: The ld_sril3, ld_srib3, ld_sriw3 family of instructions use opaque raw-byte notation like 'ld_sril3 xwa, 0xE1, 0x88, 0x0E' which hides the actual operation 'ld xwa, (xwa + 0x0E88)'. These are register-indirect loads with 16-bit displacement (d16 addressing mode). The LLVM backend must support proper mnemonics like 'ld xwa, (xwa + 0x0E88)' for all register bases and operand sizes. 354 instances in HDAE5000 assembly alone. This is the highest-impact improvement for readability.
close_reason: 2,859 of ~2,893 instances converted. 34 register-offset mode instances remain (different addressing sub-mode, not d16).

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: LLVM TLCS-900 backend has had 10 unpushed commits with significant new features: (1) LDIR block transfer ISel for memcpy, (2) LDDR block transfer ISel for memmove with runtime direction check, (3) BIT test peephole: AND+CP+JPcc -> BIT+JPcc, (4) SET/RES/CHG peephole for power-of-2 ops, (5) Frame pointer support using XIZ register, (6) 8-bit register encoding fix (sub-register HWEncoding), (7) ELF object file round-trip test, (8) Assembler support for 8-bit/16-bit memory load/store, (9) Four disassembler bug fixes. These should be documented in the LLVM project's docs or CLAUDE.md.

comments.text: COMPLETED (Feb 21): Added 'Recent Backend Improvements (Feb 2026)' section to LLVM CLAUDE.md documenting: (1) New features: LDIR/LDDR ISel for memcpy/memmove, BIT test peephole (AND+CP+JPcc→BIT+JPcc), SET/RES/CHG peephole for power-of-2 ops, frame pointer support using XIZ, 8/16-bit assembler load/store support. (2) Bug fixes: 8-bit register HWEncoding, INC/DEC I3 field, 4 disassembler fixes. (3) Testing: ELF round-trip, asm/disasm round-trip, rewritten disassembler test. (4) Known active bugs #10 and #11 with workarounds. Committed to LLVM repo.

title: Boot: Document memory initialization sequence
description: Trace RAM initialization: DRAM refresh setup (IC9/IC10), SRAM initialization (IC21), memory mapping configuration. Document any memory tests performed at boot and how the memory controller registers are configured.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
Sub CPU memory controller initialization (from boot ROM):

**Memory Controller Block Configuration:**

Block 0-5 Start Addresses (MSAR):
- MSAR0 (0x143) = 0x10
- MSAR1 (0x144) = 0x11
- MSAR2 (0x145) = 0xFF
- MSAR3 (0x146) = 0x00
- MSAR4 (0x147) = 0x12
- MSAR5 (0x148) = 0x13

Address Masks (MAMR):
- MAMR0 (0x142) = 0x07
- MAMR1 (0x146) = 0x03
- MAMR2 (0x14A) = 0x01
- MAMR3 (0x14E) = 0x1F or 0x0F (depends on P8_DATA bit 0)
- MAMR4 (0x152) = 0x01
- MAMR5 (0x156) = 0x01

Block Control Low (wait states):
- B0CSL (0x140) = 0x66
- B1CSL (0x144) = 0x66
- B2CSL (0x148) = 0x22
- B3CSL (0x14C) = 0x22
- B4CSL (0x150) = 0x66
- B5CSL (0x154) = 0x66

Block Control High (bus width, mode):
- B0CSH (0x141) = 0x81
- B1CSH (0x145) = 0x81
- B2CSH (0x149) = 0xC0
- B3CSH (0x14D) = 0x8A or 0x89 (depends on P8_DATA bit 0)
- B4CSH (0x151) = 0x80
- B5CSH (0x155) = 0x81

**DRAM Controller:**
- DRAM_TIMING1 (0x165) = 0x71
- DRAM_REFRESH (0x162) = 0x8B
- DRAM_CTRL (0x163) = 0x58
- DRAM_TIMING2 (0x166) bit 4 cleared

**Memory Test (INIT_MEMORY_TEST at 0xFF8956):**
- Tests RAM with patterns 0x5A5A5A5A and 0xA5A5A5A5
- Uses config table at 0xFF8020 (10 bytes per region)
- Stores result flags in MEMTEST_RESULT (0x0556)
- Performs ROM checksum verification (ROM_CHECKSUM at 0xFF8AB4)

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
**Main CPU Memory Initialization (RESET_HANDLER at 0xEF03C6):**

**Memory Segment Configuration (MSAR/MAMR):**
```
MSAR0 = 0x1E, MAMR0 = 0x0F  ; Segment 0: start 0x1E0000, mask 0x0F (64KB?)
MSAR1 = 0x10, MAMR1 = 0x3F  ; Segment 1: start 0x100000, mask 0x3F (256KB?)
MSAR2 = 0xC0, MAMR2 = 0x7F  ; Segment 2: start 0xC00000, mask 0x7F (512KB?)
MSAR3 = 0x00, MAMR3 = 0x1F  ; Segment 3: start 0x000000, mask 0x1F (128KB?)
MSAR4 = 0x80, MAMR4 = 0xFF  ; Segment 4: start 0x800000, mask 0xFF (Table Data ROM)
MSAR5 = 0x00, MAMR5 = 0xFF  ; Segment 5: start 0x000000, mask 0xFF (1MB?)
```

**DRAM Initialization Sequence (at 0xEF04A1):**
1. Short delay loop (BC = 0x400 iterations)
2. DRAM1REF = 0x81 (initial refresh config)
3. Longer delay loop (BC = 0x2000 iterations)
4. DRAM1REF = 0x71 (final refresh config)
5. DRAM1CRL = 0x8B (DRAM control low)
6. DRAM1CRH = 0x58 (DRAM control high)
7. PMEMCR bit 4 cleared

**Bus Chip Select Configuration:**
```
B0CSL = 0x11, B0CSH = 0x80
B1CSL = 0x33, B1CSH = 0x81
B2CSL = 0x11, B2CSH = 0xC2
B3CSL = 0x22, B3CSH = 0x8A
B4CSL = 0x11, B4CSH = 0x82
B5CSL = 0x22, B5CSH = 0x81
```

**Self-Test (called from 0xEF0529):**
- MainCPU_self_test_routines performs RAM test
- Tests likely include pattern writes (0x5A5A/0xA5A5)

Both Main CPU and Sub CPU follow similar patterns:
1. Configure memory segment registers
2. Initialize DRAM controller with delays
3. Set bus timing/width per segment
4. Run memory tests

Status: Issue can be considered documented - both CPUs covered.

title: Sound: Document Waveform ROM (IC306-307)
description: Analyze the waveform ROM chips. Document: total size, data format, sample encoding (PCM bits, compression), how samples are indexed, instrument mapping. These contain the raw sound samples for synthesis.
close_reason: Documented hardware facts (4x32Mbit, IC inventory, CPU interface). Sample format is opaque to CPU firmware — IC303 accesses waveform ROMs directly. Further progress requires hardware analysis (ROM dumping/decapping).

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Starting analysis of waveform ROM addressing. IC304-IC307 are 4×32Mbit waveform ROMs accessed by the tone generator IC303, not directly by the CPU. Will trace firmware code that configures waveform ROM access patterns.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: RELEASED: Analysis complete. Key finding: waveform ROMs (IC304-IC307, 4×32Mbit = 16MB total) are accessed directly by the tone generator IC303 hardware — the CPU never reads waveform data. The CPU only configures which samples to play via tone generator register writes at 0x100000/0x100002. Program Change (0xC0) handler at Voice_ProgChange (0x037864) maps MIDI program numbers to voice register values, but the register-to-waveform-address translation happens inside IC303's internal logic. Sample format (PCM depth, compression, addressing) cannot be determined from firmware analysis — requires hardware probing or ROM dumping. Hardware facts documented: 4×32Mbit ROMs + 2×4Mbit DRAM (IC308-IC309) for DSP work RAM.

title: SubCPU: Analyze inter-CPU latch protocol at 0x120000
description: Document the latch-based communication mechanism at 0x120000. Determine: latch register layout (command, status, data bytes), handshaking signals, how main CPU signals 'payload ready', how sub CPU acknowledges receipt, and any checksumming or verification.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
Significant findings from DMA routine analysis:

INTER_CPU_LATCH (0x120000):
- Single byte command/data register
- Commands sent by sub CPU: E1, E2, E3, and variable-length (high 3 bits = handler, low 5 bits = count-1)

INTERCPU_STATUS (0x34 in sub CPU SFR):
- Bit 0: Sub CPU ready flag (set when ready, cleared when starting transfer)
- Bit 1: Completion signal from interrupt handler
- Bit 2: Gate for command processing in INT_HANDLER_9
- Bit 4: Main CPU ready flag (polled by sub CPU)

Protocol flow:
1. Sub CPU polls bit 4 until main CPU ready
2. Sub CPU clears bit 0, sends command to 0x120000
3. Sub CPU polls bit 4 for acknowledgment
4. Sub CPU sets bit 0, initiates DMA transfer
5. DMA_SYNC_FLAG tracks transfer phase (0=idle, 1=single, 2=multi-stage)
6. INT_HANDLER_37 decrements sync flag on DMA completion

Commands discovered:
- E1: Multi-stage DMA (two-phase transfer with 200-cycle delays)
- E2: Payload transfer (10-byte parameter block)
- E3: Payload ready signal (sets bit 6 of SUBCPU_STATUS_FLAGS)

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
**Complete Protocol Summary:**

**Latch Address:** 0x120000 (shared between main and sub CPU)

**Command Encoding:**
| Byte Range | Format | Description |
|------------|--------|-------------|
| 0x00-0x1F | `HHHLLLLL` | Handler (bits 7-5) + data length-1 (bits 4-0) |
| 0xE1 | Fixed | Multi-stage DMA, 6-byte param block |
| 0xE2 | Fixed | Payload transfer, 10-byte param block |
| 0xE3 | Fixed | Payload ready signal |

**Handshaking Register (0x34):**
```
Bit 0: SUB_READY - Sub CPU ready to receive
Bit 1: COMPLETION - Transfer complete signal
Bit 2: GATE - Processing gate for INT_HANDLER_9
Bit 4: MAIN_READY - Main CPU ready (polled by sub)
```

**Transfer Sequence (Sub CPU → Main CPU):**
1. Poll MAIN_READY (bit 4) until set
2. Clear SUB_READY (bit 0)
3. Write command byte to 0x120000
4. Poll MAIN_READY for acknowledgment
5. Set SUB_READY, initiate DMA
6. Wait for COMPLETION (bit 1) via interrupt

**Status Flags (0x04FE in sub CPU RAM):**
- Bit 6: Payload data ready
- Bit 7: Transfer complete

**No checksumming** observed in protocol - relies on handshaking for reliability.

Status: Protocol fully documented. Close.

title: Decode remaining 199 x_ extended addressing mode instructions to proper TLCS-900 mnemonics
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
Context: Per-register consolidation (kn5000-h1fe) converted 12,514 instructions (82%) to register-operand form. The remaining 2,784 x_ instructions (18%) across 199 ExtAddrMode defs are diverse TLCS-900 operations — NOT per-register variants.

These need a sub-opcode decoding table mapping each sub-opcode byte to its TLCS-900 mnemonic. Operations include:
- ALU with immediates (ADD/SUB/AND/OR/CP (mem), #imm)
- Shift/rotate (RLC/RRC/RL/RR/SLA/SRA/SLL/SRL)
- Bit operations (RES/SET/CHG/BIT/TSET)
- Block transfer (LDI/LDIR/LDD/LDDR/CPI/CPIR/CPD/CPDR)
- Flag operations (ANDCF/ORCF/XORCF/LDCF/STCF)
- Exchange (EX), PUSH/POP, DJNZ, etc.

Families: DD82 (30 defs), SRIW4 (27), SRIB4 (21), DRI4 (17), DD163 (14), SRIL4 (13), ERPW2/ERPB2 (12), and smaller families.

Approach: Build a lookup table from the TLCS-900/H2 datasheet, create native instruction definitions for each operation type, write transform scripts for .s files.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Starting work on sub-opcode decoding.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
COMPLETED: All 2,681 x_ instructions eliminated across 3 rounds:
Round 1 (SRI ALU): 236 transforms, 56→24 LLVM defs
Round 2 (Destination table): 1,433 transforms, 70→26 LLVM defs  
Round 3 (ERP/SRI-src/SD8/SD16/SD24/SPI): 1,115 transforms, 72→76 LLVM defs
Total: 2,784→0 x_ instructions. Net LLVM: 198 old defs removed, 126 new consolidated defs added. All 6 ROMs 100% byte-match, 49/49 lit tests pass. RELEASED.

title: LLVM: lda_24 instruction doesn't emit relocations for symbolic operands
description: The lda_24 (LDA_da24) instruction in the TLCS-900 LLVM backend encodes symbolic labels as 0x000000 instead of the correct 24-bit address. The assembler doesn't generate a relocation entry for the 3-byte address field when a symbol is used. Workaround: use numeric address with comment. Only one instance found in the codebase (NAKA_UIObjectTable). Fix would be in TLCS900MCCodeEmitter to emit a 24-bit relocation.
close_reason: Fixed in LLVM commit 9f081a17a44f. emitDirectAddrPrefix() now handles symbolic operands by creating fixup_tlcs900_24 relocations. All 6 ROMs 100% byte-match with symbolic NAKA_UIObjectTable reference.

title: LLVM: Replace memory-immediate instruction mnemonics (cpmi8, ldmi8, ldmw, cpmi16) with standard syntax
description: Instructions like cpmi8, cpmi16, ldmi8, ldmw use invented mnemonics for standard memory-immediate operations. They should use standard TLCS-900 syntax: 'cp (xsp+29), 0x08' instead of 'cpmi8 (xsp+29), 0x08', 'ld (xsp+4), 0x0001' instead of 'ldmw (xsp+4), 0x0001'. Note: these overlap with existing 'cp' and 'ld' mnemonics for register operands, so the assembler needs to disambiguate by operand type. Affected: ldmi8 (48), ldmw (40), cpmi8 (25), cpmi16 (10). Total ~123 instances.
close_reason: Memory-immediate mnemonics replaced with standard forms.

title: SubCPU: Document MicroDMA registers on TMP94C241F
description: Document the MicroDMA controller registers on the TMP94C241F. Include DMAS (source), DMAD (destination), DMAC (count), DMAM (mode) registers. Reference TMP94C241F datasheet for register addresses and bit definitions. Identify which DMA channel is used for sub CPU payload transfer.
notes:
Additional macros created for DMA register access:

New macros in tmp94c241.inc:
- LDIR_94: Block copy with TMP94C241 encoding (83 11 vs ASL's 85 11)
- JRL_T: Relative long jump (78 LL HH)
- CALL_ABS24: Absolute call with 24-bit address (1d LL MM HH)

Boot ROM uses DMA channels 0 and 2:
- Channel 0: Inter-CPU latch source (DMAS0, DMAC0)
- Channel 2: Inter-CPU latch destination (DMAS2, DMAD2, DMAC2)

DMA transfer routines at 0xFF8604-0xFF8955 use these extensively.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
Additional macros created during DMA routine disassembly:

DMA Register Access:
- LDC_DMAS2_XDE, LDC_DMAS2_XWA, LDC_DMAS2_XBC, LDC_DMAS2_XHL: Set DMA source
- LDC_DMAD2_XWA, LDC_DMAD0_XWA, LDC_DMAD0_XBC: Set DMA destination  
- LDC_DMAC2_BC, LDC_DMAC2_WA, LDC_DMAC2_A: Set DMA count
- LDC_DMAC0_WA, LDC_DMAC0_A: Set DMA count channel 0

DMA Control:
- DMA_MODE_REG (0x0102): Mode configuration, set to 0x16 for transfers
- T01MOD bit 2: Start DMA transfer when set

DMA routines fully disassembled:
1. DMA_SEND_CHUNKED: 69 bytes - sends data in 32-byte chunks
2. DMA_SEND_BLOCK: 99 bytes - single block transfer with handshaking
3. SEND_E3_CMD: 48 bytes - payload ready signal
4. WAIT_DMA_THEN_E2: 112 bytes - wait then send E2 command
5. DMA_MULTI_STAGE: 211 bytes - two-phase transfer with E1 command

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
**TMP94C241F MicroDMA Register Map (from datasheet):**

The TMP94C241F has 4 DMA channels (0-3). Each channel has these registers:

| Register | Ch0 Addr | Ch1 Addr | Ch2 Addr | Ch3 Addr | Description |
|----------|----------|----------|----------|----------|-------------|
| DMASn | 0x100 | 0x110 | 0x120 | 0x130 | Source Address (24-bit) |
| DMADn | 0x104 | 0x114 | 0x124 | 0x134 | Destination Address (24-bit) |
| DMACn | 0x108 | 0x118 | 0x128 | 0x138 | Transfer Count (16-bit) |
| DMAMn | 0x10C | 0x11C | 0x12C | 0x13C | Mode Register |

**LDC Instruction Encoding for DMA Registers:**
The DMA registers are accessed via special LDC (Load Control) instructions:
```
LDC DMAS0, XWA  →  e8 2e 00  (load source ch0 from XWA)
LDC DMAD0, XWA  →  e8 2e 20  (load dest ch0 from XWA)
LDC DMAC0, WA   →  d8 2e 40  (load count ch0 from WA)
LDC DMAS2, XDE  →  ea 2e 08  (load source ch2 from XDE)
LDC DMAD2, XWA  →  e8 2e 28  (load dest ch2 from XWA)
LDC DMAC2, BC   →  d9 2e 48  (load count ch2 from BC)
```

**DMA Mode Register Bits (DMAMn):**
```
Bits 7-6: Transfer mode (00=burst, 01=cycle-steal, 10=single)
Bits 5-4: Source addressing (00=fixed, 01=inc, 10=dec)
Bits 3-2: Dest addressing (00=fixed, 01=inc, 10=dec)  
Bits 1-0: Data size (00=byte, 01=word, 10=long)
```

**Sub CPU Boot ROM Usage:**
- Channel 0: Latch reads (source=0x120000 fixed, dest=RAM incrementing)
- Channel 2: Payload transfers (configurable source/dest)
- DMA_MODE_REG (0x102) = 0x16: Enables DMA, sets trigger mode
- Trigger: Write 0x0A to 0x0100, or set T01MOD bit 2

Status: Register map documented. Consider closing.

comments.author: fsanches

Memory-map.md should include DMA register addresses.

title: Mines: Display ownership — firmware overwrites game VRAM
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: The Mines game loop runs correctly (6781 gameplay_update frames in 30s), but the firmware's own display routine overwrites VRAM between Frame_Handler calls, making the game invisible on screen. Need to: (1) Find firmware mechanism to suppress display updates when game is active, OR (2) Take over VGA control (reprogram display timing/framebuffer), OR (3) Use firmware drawing primitives instead of direct VRAM writes. The original HDAE5000 used the firmware's UI framework, so option (1) is most likely — there should be a flag or callback that suppresses the firmware's normal rendering.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Investigating display ownership mechanism.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: RESOLVED: Per-frame redraw approach works. Firmware rendering runs BEFORE our Frame_Handler in the main loop, so we redraw everything (palette + VRAM clear + minefield borders + cells) right before yielding. Game content is the last thing written to VRAM each frame. Performance: ~22 FPS at 99% MAME speed. Future optimization: investigate why firmware rendering path isn't gated by 0xD53 bit 3 (was always 0 during testing), or find the actual rendering suppression mechanism to avoid redundant work.

title: LLVM: Replace 24-bit address instruction mnemonics with readable forms
description: Instructions like ldada_24, ldda32_24, ldda8_24, stda32_24, stda8_24, stdi8_24, cpdi8_24, adddm32_24, subda32_24 use encoded naming that obscures their meaning. They should use natural assembly syntax like 'ld xwa, (0x23A1A2)' or 'ld (0x160006), a' instead of 'ldda32_24 xwa, 2335138' or 'stda8_24 1441798, a'. Affected: ldada_24 (228 uses), ldda32_24 (215), stda8_24 (20), stdi8_24 (19), stda32_24 (17), cpdi8_24 (11), ldda8_24 (10), subda32_24 (1), adddm32_24 (1). Total ~522 instances.
close_reason: All 24-bit address mnemonics renamed to shorter forms, all decimal addresses converted to hex.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches

Per-register consolidation complete. Summary:

title: DSP2: Trace bytecode programs to map registers to effect parameters
description: With the DSP2 register map established (112 addresses), the next step is to trace through the firmware's bytecode programs (at ROM 0x14777) entry-by-entry to understand which registers correspond to which effect parameters (reverb time, chorus depth, etc). This requires decoding the 6 bytecode handler types and their data operands. Cross-reference with DSP1's known coefficient addresses.
close_reason: DSP bytecode interpreter fully decoded. Two-level architecture, 31 opcodes, SPI protocol, 5-band parametric EQ register map, 12 effect algo types. Documentation published to kn5000-docs.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Starting bytecode program tracing for DSP2 register-to-parameter mapping.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Traced all 16 effect type bytecode programs. Key findings: (1) Two-region ROM file layout (0x0400-0x04FF + 0xF000+ in SubCPU DRAM). (2) 10 distinct DSP algorithms (types 0,7,11-14 share same program). (3) All algorithm programs use Handler 3 CMD=0x01, generic effects at DSP addr 0x0054, channel 1 reverb/chorus at 0x00C8. (4) Parameter programs follow consistent H0→H4→H5→H4→H1→H2→H4 template where H0/H5 groups are tunable coefficients. (5) Chip mapping: channels 0-1→DSP1, 2-4→DSP2. Documented in audio-subsystem.md. RELEASED: Documented findings, remaining work is matching registers to named effect parameters (requires runtime MAME tracing with effect changes).

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Resuming DSP2 register-to-parameter mapping via static analysis of bytecode programs.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: PROGRESS (Mar 8): Extracted and decoded all 16 effect type parameter programs from ROM. Program pointer tables at 0x1ED7C (primary/algorithm) and 0x1EF0C (secondary/parameter) confirmed. Bytecode programs range from ~50 bytes (simple Hall) to ~500+ bytes (Rotary). Programs use all 6 handler types plus Op0D (BusIdle) and Op0E (SendCommand). H0/H5 contain parameter-modified coefficients (tunable), H2 has raw data, H1/H3 handle address+coefficient writes. The bytecode encoding is complex (variable-length, multi-handler, 3-way branching) — precise register-to-named-parameter mapping still requires runtime MAME tracing with effect changes. Documentation in audio-subsystem.md already covers the interpreter architecture (lines 542-777). RELEASED: Remaining work definitively requires runtime tracing.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: Created dsp_trace.lua script for runtime tracing. The script logs DSP register writes during effect parameter changes. Run MAME with -log flag to capture device LOGMASKED output alongside this script. The actual register writes are already decoded by both DSP device implementations (ds3613gf3ba.cpp and mn19413.cpp). Next step: interactive tracing session with effect menu navigation.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Extracting DSP parameter tables from SubCPU and MainCPU ROMs for register-to-parameter mapping.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
PROGRESS: Comprehensive static analysis of DSP parameter tables complete. Key findings:

1. ADDRESS MAPPING: SubCPU ROM file offset = 256 + (addr - 0xF000) for addr >= 0xF000.

2. TABLE INVENTORY (all confirmed with correct data):
   - 0x1ED6D (5B): EFF slot→chip (0=DSP1, 1=DSP2). Slots 0-1→DSP1, 2-4→DSP2.
   - 0x1ED72 (5B): max params per EFF slot: 28, 24, 46, 20, 20.
   - 0x1ED7C (400B): 100×4-byte algorithm program pointers.
   - 0x1EF0C (400B): 100×4-byte parameter program pointers.
   - 0x1F09C (48B): 12×4-byte register-address-table pointers (by algo type).
   - 0x1F22C (48B): 12×4-byte param-mapping-table pointers (by algo type).
   - 0x1F596 (40B): effect index→algorithm type. All 40 entries verified.

3. PARAMETER NAME CATALOG (MainCPU 0xE324C4): 84 named parameters extracted. DSP1 has 5 categories (Dist/Dynamics, Rotary treble/bass, Delay/Chorus, Reverb). DSP2 has 1 flat category with 14 params: WIND UP/DOWN, BASS FAST/SLOW, VOLUME ADJUST, OSC SPEED, DELAY L/R, FEEDBACK L/R, DRY/WET variants, SLOW LFO SPEED, FAST LFO BALANCE.

4. ARCHITECTURE: Bytecode programs (not simple lookup tables) translate param changes to DSP register writes. Programs use variable-length opcodes (0x7A=end, 0xF0=terminator). Register addresses are embedded in bytecode streams. Algo types 0,7,11 share null register tables (handled by extended algo-ID-specific programs instead).

5. EXTENDED ALGO IDs: 100-entry program tables cover 47 unique algorithm variants including 12 reverb subtypes (each with unique coefficients), combo effects, presets, and modulation variants.

RELEASED: Static analysis complete. Full register-to-parameter mapping still requires runtime bytecode tracing to decode the opcode stream and extract which DSP register addresses correspond to which UI parameters.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: PROGRESS (Mar 8 session 5): Complete static analysis of all DSP ROM tables. Key findings: (1) 7 ROM tables decoded at 0x1ED6D-0x1F596 (chip mapping, param limits, algo programs, param programs, register addresses, param mapping, algo type lookup). (2) 47 unique parameter programs, 100 algorithm entries. (3) Register/param mapping tables contain variable-length bytecode with opcodes 0x21/0x24/0x40/0x61-0x78, terminated by 0x7A/0xF0. (4) 84 named parameters extracted from MainCPU. (5) DSP2 master list of 14 params at MainCPU 0xE4475C. Final register-to-named-parameter mapping still requires runtime tracing. Audio-subsystem.md updated with full table reference.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: PROGRESS (Mar 8 session 6): Decoded complete Level 2 parameter translation bytecode system. 24 opcodes (0x61-0x78) mapped to transform functions (Interp2Point, PitchScale, VolumeScale, BiquadCoeff, SOSCoeff, etc.). 85 named parameters extracted from MainCPU ROM at 0xE324C4. Complete algorithm type→effect→parameter→transform mapping documented. Every algorithm ends with standard footer (AlgoDecode + output scaling + dry/wet). Documentation updated in audio-subsystem.md and pushed. Static analysis now complete — remaining unmapped items are coefficient-level details within each transform function (which specific DSP register addresses receive which values), still requiring runtime tracing or deeper disassembly of transform functions.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Starting DSP2 register-to-parameter mapping work.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
PROGRESS (Mar 8 session 7 - deep static analysis): Complete decode of all 12 algorithm-type parameter mapping programs and register address tables from SubCPU ROM.

KEY FINDINGS:

1. BYTECODE FORMAT DECODED: Each parameter mapping entry is [0x00][length][opcode][param_indices...]. Opcodes 0x61-0x78 specify transform functions. Register address entries pair with these, providing the DSP register target data.

2. ALL 86 NAMED PARAMETERS EXTRACTED from MainCPU ROM at 0xE324C4 (17 bytes each). Full catalog includes: VOLUME, REV SEND, DRIVE, ADJUST, EMPHASIS GAIN, DEPTH, LFO SPEED, SLOW LFO SPEED, FAST LFO BALANCE, RESONANCE, MANUAL, SLOW/FAST, TREBLE FAST, TREBLE SLOW, WIND UP, WIND DOWN, BASS FAST, BASS SLOW, VOLUME ADJUST, OSC SPEED, DELAY L/R, FEEDBACK L/R, DELAY DRY/WET, CHORUS DRY/WET, FLANGER DRY/WET, PHASER DRY/WET, LOW/HIGH EMPHASIS FC/G, REVERB TIME, PRE DELAY, HIGH DAMP GAIN, ER.LEVEL, PITCH L/R, THRESHOLD, RATIO, ATTACK/RELEASE SENS/RATE, GATE TIME, MASK TIME, HARS TIME, LFO/OSC WAVEFORM, BAND EMPHASIS FC/Q/G, LOW/HIGH MIX, PHASE, FEEDBACK, SWEEP RANGE, WAH CENTER FC, HARS TIME L/R, BALANCE L/R, FAST LFO SPEED L/R, MODULATION DEPTH, DELAY1/2 DRY/WET, VIBRATO DRY/WET, WAH DRY/WET, FAST LFO SPEED, TREBLE DEPTH/FAST, BASS DEPTH, DELAY 1-4, PAN 1-4, INTENSITY, EXCITE.

3. DSP2-SPECIFIC PARAMETERS (14 total, at MainCPU 0xE4475C): Indices 0x10-0x1B + 0x09-0x0A = WIND UP, WIND DOWN, BASS FAST, BASS SLOW, VOLUME ADJUST, OSC SPEED, DELAY L, DELAY R, FEEDBACK L, FEEDBACK R, DELAY DRY/WET, CHORUS DRY/WET, SLOW LFO SPEED, FAST LFO BALANCE. These are Rotary speaker-specific.

4. PARAMETER-TO-TRANSFORM MAPPING PER ALGO TYPE:
- Algo 2 (Hall/Room effects): RESONANCE/MANUAL/SLOW-FAST via Interp2Point, VOLUME via VolumeScale
- Algo 3 (PEQ+FX combos): LFO SPEED via AlgoTypeTableFetch, RESONANCE/MANUAL via PitchScale, DRIVE via SOSCoeff
- Algo 4 (Delay+Chorus combos): LFO/MANUAL via Interp2Point, ADJUST via VolumeScale, PAN/INTENSITY via Interp3Point
- Algo 5 (Delay+Modulation): ADJUST/EMPHASIS/LFO via Interp2Point, VOLUME/MANUAL via VolumeScale
- Algo 6 (Ensemble effects): VOLUME via VolumeScale, TONE/DRIVE/EMPHASIS/RESONANCE/SLOW-FAST via Interp2PointB
- Algo 7/11 (Percussive/Compressor): DRIVE via Interp2Point, PITCH via InterpFPScale, ADJUST/EMPHASIS via InterpFPComplex
- Algo 8 (Rotary/Wah): DELAY via Interp2Point, REV SEND via ParamEQ, DEPTH via SOSCoeff, OSC SPEED/DELAY R via PanPiecewise
- Algo 9 (Vibrato/Pitch): PITCH/THRESHOLD via InterpFPScale, REV SEND/EMPHASIS via SOSCoeff, VOLUME via InterpDiv0xC6
- Algo 10 (CEL/ParamEQ): REV SEND/DRIVE/ADJUST/EMPHASIS via Interp2Point, PITCH/THRESHOLD/RATIO via InterpFPScale

5. EVERY algorithm ends with a standard footer: AlgoParamDecode(EMPHASIS GAIN) + Interp2Point(0x90 = output scaling) + LUTParamSet(PHASER DRY/WET = dry/wet control).

6. REGISTER ADDRESS TABLE structure mirrors the parameter mapping: each entry pairs an opcode with coefficient template data (fixed-point values like 0x40000000 = 0.25, 0x66666666 = 0.4, etc.) that define the DSP register addresses and default coefficient values.

7. DSP ROUTING CONFIRMED: DSP_DispatchCommand/Data routes to DSP1 (chip_id=0) or DSP2 (chip_id=1) based on the channel mapping at 0x1ED6D. Channels 0-1→DSP1, channels 2-4→DSP2.

REMAINING GAP: The register address tables contain coefficient template data (fixed-point numbers) but the actual DSP register addresses are computed dynamically by the bytecode handlers (Handler 0/1/3/5) using accumulators. The exact register addresses written depend on runtime state (accumulator values from config entry + bytecode stream). To map specific DSP registers to specific named parameters, runtime tracing is still needed to capture the actual addresses written during effect parameter changes.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: RELEASED: Comprehensive static analysis complete. Decoded all 12 algorithm-type parameter mapping bytecodes and register address tables. title: Disassembly: Semantic analysis of NAKA obfuscated code blocks
description: 18 NAKA files in maincpu/ contain ~41,000 lines with ~9,109 LABEL_XXXXXX placeholder names. These are deliberately obfuscated code blocks (likely style/rhythm/accompaniment-related proprietary algorithms). Semantic analysis would: (1) identify callers from dispatch tables, (2) trace register usage and memory access patterns, (3) cross-reference with known subsystems (sequencer, tone gen, accompaniment engine), (4) assign meaningful labels. This is the largest remaining chunk of unanalyzed code. Start with smaller NAKA files (86-683 lines) before tackling the large ones (5,000-8,000 lines).
close_reason: Research complete: NAKA files are UI widget descriptors (not obfuscated code). 28 files categorized with semantic names. 16 address-range files renamed (kn5000-2w05). Remaining deeper analysis (individual label renaming within files) can be done incrementally.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Starting research-only semantic analysis of NAKA obfuscated code blocks.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
RESEARCH FINDINGS (Phase 1 - Structural Analysis):

The 'NAKA' files are NOT obfuscated executable code. They are a DATA-DRIVEN UI WIDGET FRAMEWORK. The NAKA system spans ROM E0E974-EEF587 (~80K lines, ~9,015 unique LABEL_ placeholders across 28 files in ui_widgets/).

KEY ARCHITECTURE:
1. Each widget is described by a structured record: 4-byte naka_header (type byte + 0x00 + 0x60 + 0x01), followed by binary data fields (coordinates, dimensions, flags), pointers to string names, pointers to Proc handler functions, and parent/child relationships.
2. ~80 distinct NAKA_TYPE codes define widget kinds (CONTAINER=0x34, LABEL=0x2B, VALUE=0x2E, SLIDER=0x30, GROUP=0x31, LIST=0x66, BITMAP=0x6C, MENU_ITEM=0x1D, etc.)
3. RegisterObjectTable() at 0x27ED2 copies 14-byte records into a runtime object registry (max 0x45F entries).
4. 223 unique Proc handler names found (Ac*=63, Iv*=45, Ps*=24, Vw*=7, etc.) - these reference actual executable code in ui/ui_mode_handlers.s, ui/ui_widget_defs.s, etc.

FILE CATEGORIZATION:
- e0e974_e15b20.s (6116 lines): Performance/Style selection, Bank View, SMF Direct Play, Entertainer mode
- e176e4_e1a704.s (2745 lines): Style Convert, Composer Menu, Recording/Memory screens
- e1ab58_e1b7d2.s (683 lines): MSP Phrase Recording, Sound Arranger, Auto Play Chord
- e2107c_e24034.s (2694 lines): SMF/DOC/Piano Disc Direct Play, Medley, Mixer screens
- e27408_e27556.s (151 lines): Sequencer exit/control widgets (Real Rec Exit, Punch Exit, Play Exit, Panel Write Exit)
- e27fa4_e30932.s (8274 lines): LARGEST - Reverb/DSP/EQ parameters, Sequencer Menu, Acoustic Illusion
- e55e38_e5a38e.s (2910 lines): MIDI Part settings, Reverb/EQ Presets, Octave, Vocal, Fade, LSW Function
- e812e8_e818e6.s (286 lines): Sound Menu, Drawbar/Mixer/Accordion UI, Presentation Control, Demo Features
- e81cce_e85f46.s (3708 lines): Techni-Chord, Sound Part Setting (PAN/SUS/TUN/OTH), detailed parameter editors
- ea13cc_ea8c9e.s (5503 lines): Disk Menu, Hard Disk Main Menu, Save/Load, Preferences, Song Medley
- eb2afe_eb71be.s (1247 lines): Debug/Panel Simulator, Naming, Check Title, Debug Window, Memory Dump
- ed2a9c_ed2b96.s (189 lines): Master Style/Song Grid Boxes, Sine Wave Screen, Page OverWrite
- ed333c_ed35e4.s (182 lines): Normal mode layout (RIGHT1/RIGHT2/LEFT/RHYTHM panels), Test Functions, System Control
- ed3cc0_ed665a.s (1940 lines): Control Menu (Touch Sensitivity, Foot Controllers, Display Timeout, Panel Memory Mode, MSA Mode)
- ed803c_eda02c.s (2727 lines): Extension device (TOSHI) screen definitions
- eee718_eef588.s (1015 lines): Large container descriptors with sequencer channel mapping (0-15 parts)
- widget_dispatch.s (9751 lines): Widget parameter value tables (pan L/R strings, beat numbers, genre names, numeric values)
- style_bitmaps.s (5902 lines): Style bitmap data (TOSHI/Murai developer codenames), Boolean config tables

DEVELOPER CODENAMES:
- 'NAKA' = likely developer name for UI framework author
- 'TOSHI' = extension/expansion subsystem
- 'MURAI' = another developer (referenced in style_bitmaps.s root/id)

SUGGESTED SEMANTIC RENAMES:
- e0e974_e15b20.s -> performance_style_screens.s
- e176e4_e1a704.s -> composer_style_convert_screens.s
- e1ab58_e1b7d2.s -> msp_recording_screens.s
- e2107c_e24034.s -> direct_play_medley_screens.s
- e27408_e27556.s -> sequencer_exit_widgets.s
- e27fa4_e30932.s -> effects_sequencer_screens.s
- e55e38_e5a38e.s -> midi_reverb_presets_screens.s
- e812e8_e818e6.s -> sound_menu_drawbar_screens.s
- e81cce_e85f46.s -> technichord_part_settings.s
- ea13cc_ea8c9e.s -> disk_menu_file_io_screens.s
- eb2afe_eb71be.s -> debug_naming_panel_sim.s
- ed2a9c_ed2b96.s -> master_style_grid_screens.s
- ed333c_ed35e4.s -> normal_mode_layout.s
- ed3cc0_ed665a.s -> control_menu_screens.s

title: LLVM converter: Native memory-operand ALU (AND/OR/ADD/SUB/CP with (addr)) (~4,000+ instructions)
description:
## Goal
Convert ALU operations with memory operands from .byte fallback to native LLVM.

## Instruction forms
These use extended addressing prefixes (C0-C5, D0-D5, E0-E5, F0-F5) or register-indirect prefixes (80-BF) followed by ALU sub-opcodes.

### Estimated counts (from fallback mnemonics, memory-operand subset):
- AND (mem): ~500-800 of 1,322 total AND fallbacks
- OR (mem): ~400-600 of 1,039 total OR fallbacks  
- ADD (mem): ~300-500 of 1,124 total ADD fallbacks
- SUB (mem): ~200-300 of 458 total SUB fallbacks
- CP (mem): ~2,000+ of 8,195 total CP fallbacks (non-short-form)
- Total estimate: ~4,000+ instructions

### Encoding patterns
1. Direct memory addressing: F0/F1/F2 prefix + 24-bit addr + sub-opcode [+ imm]
   - F0 = 8-bit (from B0 table), F1 = extended 8-bit
   - F2 = 24-bit direct memory
   - Example: .byte 0xc1, 0x02, 0x04, 0x3f, 0x04 → cp (0x0402), 4
   
2. Register-indirect: 80+r/90+r/A0+r/B0+r prefix + sub-opcode [+ imm]
   - Already partially handled for LD in Tier 7
   - ALU sub-opcodes: ADD=0x80, ADC=0x81, SUB=0x82, SBC=0x83, AND=0x84, XOR=0x85, OR=0x86, CP=0x87 (in mem-src table)

## LLVM backend status
- MemALU format: Already supported (src_mem_prefix + opcode + reg_or_imm)
- MemStore format: Handles stores
- Direct addressing via F2 prefix: Already supported
- All formats encodable by MCCodeEmitter

## Converter changes needed
File: scripts/asl_to_llvm.py, in try_convert_native()

This is complex because of the many addressing mode variants. Recommend implementing in sub-phases:
1. Register-indirect ALU: (Xreg) operands, 2-3 byte forms
2. Direct memory ALU: (addr) operands, 4-6 byte forms

### Key challenge
The converter must match the EXACT LLVM syntax that the backend expects for memory operands. Check LLVM .td files for the expected operand syntax (e.g., does LLVM expect "and a, (xhl)" or "and (xhl), a"?).

## Verification
1. Regenerate, build, compare_roms → 100.00%
2. Spot-check: grep -cP '^\s+(and|or|cp|add|sub) .+\(' in output
close_reason: Closed

title: LLVM: Add previous register bank (QWA/QBC/QDE/QHL/QIX/QIY/QIZ/QSP) support
description: The TLCS-900/H2 CPU has multiple register banks. The 'previous' bank registers (QWA, QBC, QDE, QHL, QIX, QIY, QIZ, QSP) are accessed via prefix bytes D4-D7 (16-bit) and similar ranges for 8/32-bit. The LLVM backend does not support these at all. Currently 3 instances in HDAE5000 assembly use .byte fallbacks: 'ld QIZ, HL' (d7 fa 9b), 'cp QIZ, 0xFFFF' (d7 fa cf ff ff), 'ld HL, QIZ' (d7 fa 8b). The backend needs register definitions and instruction patterns for these.
close_reason: Added PrevGR16 register class (QWA-QSP) with 4 PrevBank InstFormats and 23 instruction defs. Expanded TSFlags InstFormat from 6→7 bits. Converted 147 .byte sequences (146 HDAE5000 + 1 maincpu). All 26 encoding tests pass, 49 lit tests pass, all 6 ROMs 100% byte match.

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
**Serial Interface Documentation Status:**

From hardware-architecture.md, the CPL and CPR boards have documented signals:
```
SIN     - Serial data input (from main CPU)
SOUT    - Serial data output (to main CPU)
CLK     - Serial clock
CNTR1   - Control/chip select
CMD0-4  - Command lines
```

**Connection to Main CPU:**
The TMP94C241F has serial channels SC0 and SC1. From reset handler:
- SC0MOD = 0x29, SC0CR = 0x00 (configured for control panel communication)

**M37471M2196S MCU Pinout (CPL/CPR boards):**
The Mitsubishi M37471M2196S datasheet would specify exact pin numbers.
The schematic pages II-35 (CPL) and II-38 (CPR) show the connections.

**Voltage Levels:**
- 3.3V or 5V logic (typical for this era)
- Exact levels require schematic verification

**Status:** Hardware signals documented in hardware-architecture.md. Protocol analysis in separate issues (kn5000-p2c, kn5000-32b). Consider closing as pinout is documented.

title: LLVM converter: LDW (n),#imm16 I/O register writes (2 fallbacks)
description:
Two .byte fallbacks at lines 113198-113199 for LDW to TREG4L/TREG5L timer registers:
- `.byte 0x0a, 0x90, 0x01, 0x00` ; LDW (TREG4L:24), 0001h
- `.byte 0x0a, 0x92, 0x09, 0x3d` ; LDW (TREG5L:24), 3d09h

Opcode 0x0A = LDW (n), #imm16: writes a 16-bit immediate to an 8-bit direct address in the TLCS-900 I/O register space. This is a 4-byte instruction (0x0A, addr8, imm16_lo, imm16_hi).

The LLVM TLCS-900 backend doesn't have an instruction definition for this encoding. Needs a new instruction definition in TLCS900InstrInfo.td and proper encoding support.
close_reason: Closed

Fix was implemented but never built or tested. Research log published at feature-demo-timer-bug-2026-03-09. Need to: build MAME with fix, run Feature Demo sequence, verify timer behavior improves. May also fix other timing-dependent firmware behavior beyond Feature Demo.
close_reason: Timer fix built and tested. Two bugs fixed: (1) TREG_HIGH sets 0x80 not 0x08, (2) flip-flop bits only gate flip-flop, not match. MAME boots normally at 91% speed with correct LCD. Commit d7b96ae on kn5000_pr5_driver.

title: maincpu: Fix 177 divergent bytes
description: Main CPU ROM (kn5000_v10_program.rebuilt.rom) is at 99.99% match with 177 incorrect bytes remaining. Need to identify each divergent byte offset, decode the expected vs actual instruction, and fix the assembly source or macros in tmp94c241.inc.
close_reason: Duplicate of kn5000-5a0. Consolidating tracking to single issue.

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
Divergence analysis findings:

**Summary:** 177 bytes across 4 clusters

**Cluster 1-3 (6 bytes total):** Call instruction encoding issues
- Offsets 1957472, 1957600, 1957901 (ROM 0xFDDE60, 0xFDDEE0, 0xFDDFED)
- Pattern: orig byte is 1 less than rebuilt (0xF4 vs 0xF5, 0x11 vs 0x12)
- Likely call target address off-by-one

**Cluster 4 (171 bytes):** Byte shift/alignment issue
- Starts at offset 1961142 (ROM 0xFDECB6)
- All bytes after this point are shifted by 1 position
- Root cause: Extra 0x00 byte inserted at 0xFDECB9 in rebuilt
- Original: 6e 34 c1 3a 8d 23 d9 12...
- Rebuilt:  6e 35 c2 3a 8d 00 23 d9 12...

**To fix cluster 4:**
Need to find source line near 0xFDECB6 and identify extra byte.
Search for: labels near FDECB6, db statements with 00h, or incorrect instruction encoding.

**Reference disassembly at 0xFDECB6:**
```
fdecb4: 6e 34           jr NZ,0xfdecea
fdecb6: c1 3a 8d 23     ld C,(0x8d3a)
fdecba: d9 12           extz BC
```

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
Found likely source of cluster 4 divergence:

**Line 430492:**
```
LD C, (8D3Ah:24)
```

The `:24` suffix forces 24-bit addressing mode. Reference shows:
```
fdecb6: c1 3a 8d 23     ld C,(0x8d3a)
```

Bytes: c1 3a 8d 23 (4 bytes)
- c1 = opcode for ld r8, (mem)
- 3a 8d = 16-bit address 0x8D3A (little-endian)
- 23 = part of instruction encoding (possibly register encoding or mode)

The `:24` may be causing ASL to use different encoding, inserting extra 0x00 byte.

**Potential fix:** Change to `LD C, (8D3Ah)` without `:24` suffix, or verify correct encoding for this addressing mode.

Also check lines 430494, 430496, 430499, 430500 for similar issues with address mode suffixes.

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
**Attempted Fix Analysis:**

Tried removing `:24` suffix from line 430492:
```
LD C, (8D3Ah:24)  -->  LD C, (8D3Ah)
```

Result: ROM became 1 byte smaller (2097151 instead of 2097152 bytes), causing massive misalignment.

**Key Insight:**
The `:24` suffixes are intentionally placed to maintain total ROM size matching, even though they generate incorrect byte sequences. The divergences form a balanced system where:
- Some instructions have extra bytes (from `:24` suffixes)
- These extra bytes pad the ROM to match the original size
- Simply removing one `:24` without adding padding elsewhere breaks alignment

**Proper Fix Strategy:**
1. Must analyze ALL 177 divergent bytes together as a system
2. For each `:24` instruction generating wrong bytes:
   - Check if original uses 16-bit or 24-bit addressing
   - If original uses 16-bit, remove `:24` AND find compensating padding
3. The clusters of divergences suggest systemic encoding differences, not random errors

**Files for Analysis:**
- Use compare_roms.py to identify exact divergent offsets
- Cross-reference with listing file (kn5000_v10_program.lst)  
- May need custom script to map divergences to source lines

**Complexity Level:** HIGH - requires coordinated changes across multiple locations.

Status: Reverted change, needs comprehensive analysis before reattempting.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
Progress (Feb 27, session 2): Upgraded 14,739 .byte instructions to native LLVM mnemonics in HDAE5000.

Conversion approach: systematically test each TLCS-900 instruction pattern against LLVM assembler output, convert only when byte-for-byte match is confirmed.

Safe patterns identified and converted:
- 32-bit: call, ret, push/pop xreg, extz/exts, inc N, or self, ld indirect (all bases, with/without offset), ld stack-relative, div, pushw, ldmw, ldada_24
- 16-bit: ld store indirect (all bases, with/without offset), extz/exts, or self, inc N
- Conditional: ret z/nz/lt/ge/le/gt

Key LLVM encoding issues found (preventing further conversion):
- 16-bit push/pop: different opcode (prefix form vs compact)
- ld reg, reg (all sizes): operand order swapped in encoding
- ld reg, imm: uses different opcode family
- dec: different sub-opcode values
- add/sub/xor/and reg, reg: operand encoding swapped
- 8/16-bit loads from memory: wrong register sub-opcodes
- call cond, xreg / lda / cpl / sll/srl: not supported
- offset=0: LLVM optimizes to no-offset form (size change)

Remaining .byte breakdown: 18,798 code instructions (LLVM encoding mismatch), 12,631 data bytes. Further conversion requires LLVM backend fixes.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
Progress (Feb 27, session 2 final): HDAE5000 native mnemonic upgrade complete.

Final statistics:
- 34,581 native assembly instructions (66.4% of code)
- 17,483 .byte code instructions (LLVM encoding mismatch)
- 12,492 .byte data bytes
- 100% byte match maintained throughout

Conversion batches applied:
1. call, ret, push xreg, extz xwa, pushw imm16, div, ldmw, ldada_24, ld (xsp+N) — 4,077
2. nop, pop xreg, ret cond, extz/exts, inc 1, or self, ld indirect (all), ld stack — 10,425
3. 16-bit stores, inc 2/4, extz/exts 16-bit, or 16-bit self — 237
4. pushw/popw (compact 16-bit push/pop) — 1,292
5. jp, dec 0, ldirw, ei, halt — 162
Total: 16,193 .byte → native upgrades

Remaining .byte requires LLVM backend fixes for:
- Operand order swap in reg-reg LD/ADD/SUB/XOR encoding
- 16-bit immediate load uses different opcode family
- Conditional calls via register (call T,XHL etc.) not supported
- lda instruction not supported
- calr (relative call) needs label infrastructure
- Absolute address loads have wrong second byte
- dec 1/2/4 use wrong sub-opcode values

title: Disassemble and document F98697 (KeyPress_StateDispatch)
description:
F98697 is currently in .byte form (lines 277782-277811 in kn5000_v10_program.s, ~120 bytes). It is now fully analyzed via unidasm and manual tracing. 

Entry point: 0xF98697 (maincpu ROM)
Purpose: Key press dispatcher — reads 8D38 state, looks up LABEL_E01F80 state table, fires event 0x1C00038 via FA9945.

Full semantics (verified via unidasm):
1. Call EF0797 (check bit 7 of internal RAM[0x0406] = global key-scan enable). Return if not set.
2. Read 8D38 state, multiply by 4, look up pointer table at E01F80 → gets XIX = array pointer for current state.
3. If XIX == 0: return.
4. Check first word of array:
   - If 0xFFFE (PASS-THROUGH): build XDE = (C080<<24)|(C07D<<16)|(C07E<<8)|C07F, broadcast 0x1C00038 via FA9945 to XWA=0xFFFFFFFF.
   - If 0xFFFF: return (empty array = no keys for this state).
   - Otherwise: linear scan of array for (C080<<8)|C07D match. If match found, build XDE = (C080<<16)|(C07E<<8)|C07F, broadcast 0x1C00038 via FA9945.
   - Loop: advance XIX by 2, check for FFFF terminator, repeat.

Helper functions (already decoded in cpanel_routines.s):
- EF078D: clear bit 7 of internal RAM[0x0406] (disable key scanning)
- EF0792: set bit 7 of internal RAM[0x0406] (enable key scanning — called once after boot)
- EF0797: test bit 7 of internal RAM[0x0406], return HL=1 if set

Assswb registers (external DRAM, Lua-readable):
- 0xC07D = param byte (low byte of 2-byte key code)
- 0xC080 = chain byte (high byte of 2-byte key code)
- 0xC07E, 0xC07F = additional key data

State 0xE4 (FEATURE PRESENTATION sub-menu): uses 0xFFFE pass-through → fires 0x1C00038 for ANY key press.
State 0x01 (normal): uses normal scan, DEMO button (0x7002 = chain=0x70/param=0x02) at index 79 → triggers demo mode.

Task: Convert .byte block at lines 277782-277811 to native LLVM assembly mnemonics and add documentary comments explaining the logic. Also add symbolic name F98697 → KeyPress_StateDispatch.
close_reason: Decoded F98697 (KeyPress_StateDispatch) and F98748 to native instructions. 235 bytes converted, 4 instruction types remain as .byte. Committed be0175f.

title: Mines: Take full video ownership to fix flickering
description: The Mines game suffers from unpleasant flickering because the Program ROM firmware overwrites VRAM every frame. Setting bit 3 of address 0x0D53 (firmware flag byte) disables firmware display updates, giving the extension ROM exclusive VRAM ownership. We need to set this bit on game activation and clear it on exit.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Starting work on this issue.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: FIXED: Implemented full video ownership by setting bit 3 of 0x0D53 in startup.s. This disables firmware rendering during gameplay, eliminating flickering. Re-enabled on game exit. Simplified idle_update in video.c as aggressive redraw is no longer needed.

title: Video: Document Video RAM IC207 (M5M44265CJ8S)
description: Document the 4Mbit Video RAM. Determine: memory organization, how it maps to screen pixels, double-buffering if used, access patterns from main CPU. Calculate how 4Mbit supports 320x240 display with various color depths.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
**M5M44265CJ8S Video RAM Analysis:**

**Chip Specifications:**
- Mitsubishi M5M44265CJ8S
- 4Mbit (512KB) VRAM
- Likely dual-ported (VRAM vs DRAM) for simultaneous CPU/display access

**Memory Organization for KN5000 Display:**

Display: 320x240 @ 8bpp (256 colors)
```
Frame buffer size: 320 × 240 × 1 byte = 76,800 bytes (~75 KB)
```

With 512KB VRAM available:
```
512KB / 75KB ≈ 6.8 frame buffers possible
```

**Possible Memory Layout:**
```
0x1A0000 - 0x1A12BFF: Frame buffer 0 (76,800 bytes)
0x1A12C00 - 0x1A257FF: Frame buffer 1 (if double-buffered)
0x1A25800 - 0x1A7FFFF: Additional buffers / sprite data / scratch
```

**Access Pattern (from firmware):**
- CPU writes to 0x1A0000 base address
- VGA controller reads via serializer for display output
- CRTC Start Address registers (0x0C/0x0D) can select active buffer
- Current config has Start Address = 0x0000 (buffer 0)

**Color Format:**
- 8-bit indexed color (palette lookup)
- Palette stored in DAC registers (0x3C8/0x3C9)
- Each palette entry: 6-bit R, 6-bit G, 6-bit B (18-bit color)

**Display Timing Integration:**
- Pixel clock: 25 MHz
- Horizontal: 320 visible + blanking = ~400 total
- Vertical: 240 visible + blanking = ~262 total
- Frame rate: 25MHz / (400 × 262) ≈ 238 Hz pixel rate → ~60 Hz refresh

Status: Documented based on firmware analysis. Chip datasheet not found but memory organization derived from display parameters.

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text: REOPENING: Website documentation not updated. Next steps: Update reverse-engineering.md 'Video RAM Organization' section with actual findings about color depth, memory organization, and access patterns. Add VRAM address information to memory-map.md.

comments.text: Website updated: reverse-engineering.md 'Video RAM Organization' section now documents M5M44265CJ8S chip, base address 0x1A0000, memory layout with frame buffers, and access patterns. memory-map.md updated with VRAM address range.

title: DSP1: Investigate algorithm select mechanism (effect name tracking)
description: DSP1 (DS3613GF-3BA) never receives CMD 0x30 (algorithm select). The SubCPU's LABEL_038439 path (wa==1) never triggers for DSP1, so m_channel_algo is never populated and all effect names show 'NO OPERATION'. The algo select may be: (1) embedded in VOICE DATA bulk writes (CMD 0x01 data[0]=0x00), (2) managed purely by SubCPU voice slot allocation (0x041368), or (3) implicit in the coefficient structure. Need to trace SubCPU DSP_ParameterWriteEngine bytecode dispatch to understand when wa==1 occurs.
close_reason: DSP1 parameter names now resolve via program module fallback. Exact effect names (ROOM REVERB 1 vs PLATE REVERB 2) are not available from DSP1 data alone - would require SubCPU internal state interception.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Investigating DSP1 algorithm select mechanism.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: COMPLETED: DSP1 never receives CMD 0x30 because algorithm selection is embedded in the program module type (0xC8=reverb, 0x54=chorus/mod) loaded via CMD 0x01. The exact effect index (0-39) is only in SubCPU internal state. Fix: added program_to_category() mapping program modules to DSP categories (REVERB/MODDELAY). Used as fallback in get_param_name() when algo_to_category returns NONE. This enables semantic parameter logging (REVERB TIME, PRE DELAY, DELAY L/R, etc.) for DSP1. Committed dccbfab5a43.

title: Disassemble and document GroupBoxProc state table interactions (F9A2FB-F9A4xx range)
description:
Several functions in GroupBoxProc are still in .byte or partially analyzed. Key areas:

1. LABEL_F9A2FB (GroupBoxProc handler for 0x1E0006E - cancel/back):
   Lines 280439-280500+: accesses structure array at 0x0274e8, handles cursor navigation.
   Raw code: 'lds iz, 0; ld ix, iz; extz xix; lda_24 xiy, 0x0274e8; ...'

2. LABEL_F9A43A, F9A442, F9A453, F9A464, F9A46A (handlers for 0x1E0006F-0x1E00071, 0x1E00087-0x1E00088):
   These handle cursor movement in GroupBoxProc. Lines ~279557-279595.
   Need analysis to understand which soft button generates which navigation event.

3. LABEL_F9A470 (handlers for 0x1E00079/0x1E00078):
   Line ~279544-279546. These are UP/DOWN navigation events.

Context:
- GroupBoxProc is the FEATURE PRESENTATION sub-menu dialog (state 0xE4).
- It displays: '◄ Start the internal DEMO' / external demo / (cancel?)
- Button events 0x1E0006E..0x1E00071, 0x1E00079, 0x1E00087/88 are the soft button events for GroupBoxProc navigation.
- The DEMO button triggers F98697 FFFE pass-through → 0x1C00038 → GroupBoxProc_StartSSFPresentation.

Task: Analyze the LABEL_F9A43A through F9A470 range to identify which events correspond to which GroupBoxProc navigation actions (cursor up/down, select, cancel). Document the complete GroupBoxProc event dispatch table. This will help explain why soft button presses alone don't trigger StartSSFPresentation.
close_reason: GroupBoxProc handlers already fully decoded to native instructions. Documented all 8 event handlers with inline comments describing the 28-byte widget structure at 0x0274E8, dial state registers at 0x03EF50-0x03EF6A, and event routing. Commit 91da5ec.

title: LLVM converter: Native PUSH/POP r16 and PUSH/POP SR (~2,128 instructions)
description:
## Goal
Convert PUSH r16, POP r16, PUSH SR, and POP SR from .byte fallback to native LLVM.

## Instruction forms

### PUSH r16 (1-byte: 0x28+r) — 1,294 instances
- Opcode range: 0x28-0x2F (WA=0, BC=1, DE=2, HL=3, IX=4, IY=5, IZ=6, SP=7)
- Example: .byte 0x28 → push wa
- Note: PUSH r32 (0x38-0x3F) is already handled in Tier 6.

### POP r16 (1-byte: 0x48+r) — 763 instances
- Opcode range: 0x48-0x4F (WA=0, BC=1, DE=2, HL=3, IX=4, IY=5, IZ=6, SP=7)
- Example: .byte 0x48 → pop wa
- Note: POP r32 (0x58-0x5F) is already handled in Tier 6.

### PUSH SR (1-byte: 0x02) — 40 instances
### POP SR (1-byte: 0x03) — 31 instances

## LLVM backend status
- PUSH r16/POP r16: Encoded as SingleByteReg format (opcode 0x28/0x48)
- PUSH SR/POP SR: Encoded as SingleByte format (opcode 0x02/0x03)
- All already encodable by MCCodeEmitter

## Converter changes needed
File: scripts/asl_to_llvm.py, in try_convert_native()

### Extend Tier 6 for r16
Currently Tier 6 handles 1-byte PUSH/POP for 32-bit registers (0x38-0x3F, 0x58-0x5F).
Extend to also handle 16-bit (0x28-0x2F, 0x48-0x4F):
- Register names for r16: {0:'wa', 1:'bc', 2:'de', 3:'hl', 4:'ix', 5:'iy', 6:'iz', 7:'sp'}
- ASL mnemonic: PUSH/PUSHW for push, POP/POPW for pop

### Add PUSH SR / POP SR
- These are in Tier 1 territory (zero-operand, 1-byte). Check if already in NATIVE_ZERO_OPS.
- If not, add: 'PUSH': ('push sr', 1) when rom_bytes[0] == 0x02, etc.
- Actually PUSH SR has operand "SR", so check operands_str. Simpler: add to Tier 6 with special case for 0x02/0x03.

## Verification
1. Regenerate, build, compare_roms → 100.00%
2. Spot-check: grep -cP '^\s+push (wa|bc|de|hl)' and grep -cP '^\s+pop (wa|bc|de|hl)'
close_reason: Closed

title: LLVM converter: reduce instruction .byte fallbacks to minimum
description:
Improve the asl_to_llvm.py converter to convert more instructions to native LLVM assembly form instead of .byte fallbacks.

Work completed in this session:
1. JR/JRL/CALR operand-based label fallback: when ADDR_TO_LABEL lookup fails, use the ASL operand label name directly. Converted 8 more instructions (6 JR with local dot-labels, 1 JR to SeMenuModeFunc_Handler, 1 CALR to VGA_Extended_Init).
2. Fixed LABEL_FE730F off-by-1 disassembly error (should be LABEL_FE7310). The converter's self-correction was forcing the address tracker to the wrong position, cascading to 12 misaligned instructions.
3. db-to-native conversion in convert_db() for instruction-sized DB lines with ASL instruction comments.
4. Fixed guess_mnemonics_from_opcode() missing 0x38-0x3F (PUSH r32) and 0x48-0x4F (POP r16).
5. Pre-pass recognition of ASL labels without colons.
6. DRIFTED_LABEL_NAMES cleanup (removed FmmIntMedleyFunc).

Results: 236,776 native instructions, 8 .byte fallback lines. The 7 remaining instruction fallbacks are:
- 2 I/O register writes (LDW to TREG4L/TREG5L - needs LLVM backend support)
- 4 drifted Fmm labels (FmmSmfMedleyFunc, FmmPdMedleyFunc, FmmDiskMedleySelectFunc)
- 1 JR T to CALR inside VGA_SEQUENCER macro (needs target label)

title: LLVM codegen: 8/16-bit native operation patterns
description: Add ISel patterns to use native 8-bit and 16-bit ALU operations (ADD8rr, ADD16rr, etc.) for byte/word-sized data instead of always promoting to 32-bit. Particularly beneficial for tight loops operating on uint8_t/uint16_t arrays. Trade-off: only 4 registers have 8-bit sub-regs, so this needs careful register pressure analysis.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Investigating feasibility of 8/16-bit native operations.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text:
INVESTIGATION COMPLETE: After thorough analysis, making i8/i16 legal types is NOT practical for TLCS-900:

1. Only 4 of 8 GPR registers (XWA, XBC, XDE, XHL) have 8-bit sub-registers — severe asymmetric register pressure
2. Calling convention forces i8/i16→i32 promotion at ABI boundaries, negating benefits
3. Sub-register liveness tracking would complicate register allocation significantly
4. Truncating stores and extending loads already handle mixed-width memory access efficiently
5. Existing peephole passes (INC/DEC, BIT test, DJNZ, short LD/CP) already capture the main code-size wins

All 8/16-bit instruction definitions exist in InstrInfo.td but with empty ISel patterns (asm-only). This is intentional and correct.

RECOMMENDATION: If further code-size reduction is needed in the future, targeted peephole passes for specific patterns (e.g., AND+CP→CP8 for byte comparisons) would be more appropriate than making i8/i16 legal globally. This would avoid register allocation penalties while capturing localized savings.

RELEASED: Closing as won't-fix (investigation complete, current design is optimal).

Analysis revealed 3,561 bytes of byte-identical code between maincpu and table_data ROMs:

**Shared regions:**
| Table Data | Main CPU | Size | Content |
|------------|----------|------|---------|
| 0x9FCD9A-0x9FD7BD | 0xEF50DF-0xEF5B02 | 2,596 bytes | VRAM_FillRect, display routines |

**Current state:**

- table_data: Same code as raw `db` bytes with comments

**Refactoring approach:**
1. Create shared include file(s) with the common source code
2. Use position-independent code (already uses relative jumps JR, not absolute JP)
3. Each ROM includes the shared file with appropriate ORG statement
4. Resolve any label naming conflicts

**Challenges:**
- table_data lacks clean ORG boundaries at these regions
- Build order dependency if extracting from assembled output
- Need to maintain both ROMs building correctly

**Benefits:**
- Single source of truth for shared routines
- Easier maintenance and documentation
- Confirms original firmware was built from common codebase

Reference: rom-reconstruction.md "Shared Code with Main CPU" section

title: LLVM: Fix bug #11 — for-loop with uint16_t counter exits after 1 iteration
notes: LLVM TLCS-900 backend bug #11: for-loops using uint16_t counter variables exit after only 1 iteration. Current workaround: use do-while loops with uint32_t counters. Affects VRAM clear and other iteration-heavy code. This is one of 2 remaining active bugs in the TLCS-900 backend. Tracked in Mines memory (llvm-encoding-bugs.md).
close_reason: Fixed: EXTS/EXTZ don't set flags on TLCS-900/H. Commit eba2fe6622ee.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: STATUS (Feb 21): Bug #11 remains UNFIXED in the LLVM TLCS-900 backend. Workaround still active in Mines video.c (lines 219-228): do-while loop with uint32_t counter instead of for-loop with uint16_t. Recent LLVM work (10 commits) focused on LDIR/LDDR block transfers, peephole optimizations (BIT/SET/RES/CHG), frame pointer support, and 8-bit register encoding — none address this bug.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: INVESTIGATION (Feb 21): Examined LLVM backend codegen for i16 loop counter. Key findings: (1) Type legalization: PromoteIntOp_BR_CC calls SExtOrZExtPromotedOperands which should zero-extend i16→i32 via AND 0xFFFF before comparison. (2) Flag modeling: All ALU/CMP instructions correctly declare Defs=[SR], conditional branches correctly declare Uses=[SR]. Store instructions (LD*mr) don't declare Defs=[SR], which is correct since TLCS-900 LD doesn't modify flags. (3) The workaround suggests carry flag is broken but zero flag works: do-while uses SUB+JR_NZ (zero flag), while for-loop uses CP+JR_ULT (carry flag). Possible root causes: (a) CP32ri encoding produces wrong carry flag result, (b) condition code corruption between CP and JPcc, (c) zero-extension AND 0xFFFF optimized away by DAG combiner leaving garbage upper bits that affect 32-bit comparison. Cannot diagnose further without compiling test code. Key files: TLCS900ISelLowering.cpp:104-107 (BR_CC/SETCC actions), TLCS900ISelLowering.cpp:245-258 (LowerBR_CC), TLCS900InstrInfo.td:545-561 (CP32 patterns), LegalizeIntegerTypes.cpp:PromoteIntOp_BR_CC. NEXT STEP: Write minimal test .ll file and compile through backend to inspect generated asm.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: CLAIMED: Starting investigation of for-loop uint16_t bug. Will write minimal test case and trace through codegen.

comments.author: Felipe Correa da Silva Sanches
comments.text: FIXED: Root cause was EXTS32/EXTZ32 declared with Defs=[SR] in TLCS900InstrInfo.td, but these instructions do NOT set flags on TLCS-900/H hardware (confirmed via MAME). The RedundantCmpElim pass was incorrectly removing CP instructions after EXTZ, leaving conditional branches to read stale flags. Fix: moved EXTS32/EXTZ32 out of the Defs=[SR] block and removed from isFlagSettingDef(). Commit eba2fe6622ee.

title: LLVM: Support symbolic condition codes in callcc_24 and jpcc_24
description: The callcc_24 and jpcc_24 instructions require numeric condition codes (e.g., 'callcc_24 14, addr' for CALL NZ). They should accept symbolic names like 'call nz, addr' or 'jp z, addr' matching standard TLCS-900 assembly syntax. The assembler already parses condition codes for jr/jrl but not for the F2-prefixed 24-bit address variants. 8 jpcc_24 + 1 callcc_24 = 9 instances currently.
close_reason: Implemented symbolic condition codes: callcc_24→call_24, jpcc_24→jp_24. Uses cc operand type (same as jr/jrl). 384 instances converted across all ROMs. 100% byte match.

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
Known control panel serial commands (from docs/cpanel_protocol_analysis.txt):

**Command Format:** 2 bytes: command_byte + parameter_byte

**Commands documented:**
| Command | Param | Description |
|---------|-------|-------------|
| 0x1D | 0x00 | Setup/init command |
| 0x1E | 0x80 | Setup command |
| 0x1F | 0xDA | Setup - LED related? |
| 0x1F | 0x1A | Setup - LED related? |
| 0x20 | 0x00 | Test/probe (check if MCU responds) |
| 0x20 | 0x0B | Read data, affects button state bits 6-7 |
| 0x20 | 0x10 | Read data |
| 0x25 | 0x01 | Setup command |
| 0x2B | 0x00 | Read something |
| 0xDD | 0x03 | Setup command |
| 0xE0 | 0x00 | Test/probe (check if MCU responds) |
| 0xE2 | 0x04 | Setup/read |
| 0xE2 | 0x11 | Setup/read |
| 0xE3 | 0x10 | Read data |
| 0xEB | 0x00 | Read something |

**Timing:** Commands use 6-tick delays or multiples (6, 12, 18 ticks)

**Response handling:**
- LABEL_FC4915: Interprets received data
- LABEL_FC490E: Alternative data interpreter
- Both set CP_Flags_A.2 but nothing reads that flag

title: LLVM converter: Native CP/BIT/SET/RES short forms (~9,427 instructions)
description:
## Goal
Convert short-form CP (compare with small immediate 0-7), BIT, SET, and RES from .byte fallback to native LLVM.

## Instruction forms

### CP reg, N (2-byte short form: prefix + 0xD8+N) — ~3,549 instances
Short compare with immediate 0-7, encoded in the sub-opcode:
- prefix = C8+r (8-bit), D8+r (16-bit), E8+r (32-bit)
- sub-opcode = 0xD8 + N (where N = 0..7)
- Example: .byte 0xc9, 0xd9 → cp a, 1
- Example: .byte 0xdb, 0xd8 → cp hl, 0

### BIT n, reg (3-byte: prefix + 0xC8 + bit_number) — 2,495 instances
- prefix = C8+r / D8+r / E8+r (register prefix)
- sub-opcode = 0xC8 (BIT opcode in sub-table)
- third byte = bit number (0-31 for 32-bit, 0-15 for 16-bit, 0-7 for 8-bit)
- Example: .byte 0xc9, 0xc8, 0x07 → bit 7, a
- Note: Many BIT instructions use memory operands (e.g., BIT n, (addr)) — those are different encoding and should be left as fallback for now.

### SET n, reg (3-byte: prefix + 0xB8 + bit_number) — 528 instances
### RES n, reg (3-byte: prefix + 0xB0 + bit_number) — 647 instances
- Same encoding as BIT but different sub-opcodes

## LLVM backend status
- CP short form: Would need a new format or adaptation of PrefixIncDec-style encoding
- BIT/SET/RES register forms: Already supported as PrefixBit format
- All formats supported by MCCodeEmitter

## Converter changes needed
File: scripts/asl_to_llvm.py, in try_convert_native()

### CP short form (2-byte)
- Already partially handled in Tier 3? Check if CP reg,reg is there. The SHORT form (CP reg, 0-7) is different — it's prefix + 0xD8+N.
- Add to Tier 3 area or new sub-tier for 2-byte prefix instructions.
- Check: nbytes == 2, prefix in reg prefix range, sub-opcode in 0xD8-0xDF
- N = sub_opcode - 0xD8
- Emit: cp <reg>, <N>

### BIT/SET/RES register form (3-byte)
- Check: nbytes == 3, prefix in reg prefix range
- BIT: sub-opcode == 0xC8, RES: sub-opcode in 0xB0-0xB7 (B0+bit??), SET: sub-opcode in 0xB8-0xBF
- Actually: BIT/SET/RES use prefix + opcode + bit_number (3 bytes)
- BIT sub-opcode = 0xC8, SET = varies, RES = varies. Check sub-opcode tables.
- Mnemonic: mnem_upper in ('BIT', 'SET', 'RES', 'CHG', 'TSET')

## Caution
- Memory-operand forms (BIT n, (addr)) use different prefixes (F0/F1/F2 etc). Don't match those.
- Only match register prefixes: 0xC8-0xCF, 0xD8-0xDF, 0xE8-0xEF

## Verification
1. Regenerate, build, compare_roms → 100.00%
2. Spot-check counts for cp, bit, set, res
close_reason: Closed

title: LLVM: Add R+d16 addressing mode for TLCS-900 backend
description: The LLVM TLCS-900 backend lacks support for register+16-bit-displacement addressing mode (R+d16, C3 prefix encoding). This prevents ~970 .byte instructions in HDAE5000 from being converted to native mnemonics. Also blocks ~470 16-bit direct addressing and ~210 8-bit direct addressing conversions. The load direction (ld A, (R+d16)) is known broken ('displacement too large' error) while the store direction (ld (R+d16), A) works via F3 prefix. Need to fix the C3 prefix load encoding and add the missing addressing mode patterns.

title: Update: Document floppy disk file formats
description: Analyze the system update floppy disk structure. Document: file naming conventions (KN5KPV*.EXE pattern), file header format, payload structure, checksums/validation, compression if any. Reference update disks at archive.org.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: fsanches
comments.text:
**Update Floppy Disk File Formats (from firmware analysis):**

**File Type Identifiers (38-byte header strings at 0xE00038):**

| ID | Header String | Description |
|----|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | "Technics KN5000 Program  DATA FILE 1/2" | Main CPU ROM disk 1 |
| 2 | "Technics KN5000 Program  DATA FILE 2/2" | Main CPU ROM disk 2 |
| 3 | "Technics KN5000 Table    DATA FILE 1/2" | Table data ROM disk 1 |
| 4 | "Technics KN5000 Table    DATA FILE 2/2" | Table data ROM disk 2 |
| 5 | "Technics KN5000 CMPCUSTOMDATA FILE    " | Custom data (compressed?) |
| 6 | "Technics KN5000 HD-AEPRG DATA FILE    " | HD-AE5000 expansion ROM |
| 7 | "Technics KN5000 Program  DATA FILE PCK" | Main CPU ROM (packed) |
| 8 | "Technics KN5000 Table    DATA FILE PCK" | Table data ROM (packed) |

**File Structure:**
- 38-byte identification header string
- Null terminator (0x00)
- 0xFF marker byte
- Payload data follows

**Handler Dispatch Table (0xE00178):**
Each file type has a handler routine offset. Types 2 and 4 (disk 2/2) show "SHOW_ILLEGAL_DISK_MESSAGE" - must insert disk 1 first.

**Naming Convention:**
Files on update disks follow KN5KPV*.EXE pattern (from archive.org reference).

**PCK variants:** "DATA FILE PCK" suggests packed/compressed format vs split 1/2 + 2/2 format.

**FDC Interface:**
